statement: Django uses MTV (Model-Template-View), NOT MVC. The Django 'View' is the controller — it handles business logic and selects templates. The 'Template' is the presentation layer. Models are Django's ORM layer with built-in validation. Key philosophy: batteries included — ORM, auth, admin, forms, caching, sessions all built in. UNSAID: Django's 'fat models, thin views' pattern means business logic belongs in model methods, not in views. Views that directly manipulate model data without model methods are hard to test and reuse.

Django ORM uses an Active Record pattern — each model instance represents a DB row. QuerySets are lazy: they don't hit the DB until evaluated (iteration, slicing, len(), bool()). This means chaining .filter().exclude().order_by() doesn't query the DB. UNSAID: QuerySets are ALSO cached — calling list(qs) evaluates and caches; qs[0] re-evaluates. This leads to the classic N+1 problem: iterating over objects and accessing related objects in a loop fires one query per iteration. select_related() and prefetch_related() are the fix.

The N+1 query problem: fetching 100 blog posts and then accessing post.author in a template fires 101 queries (1 for posts + 1 per post for author). Fix: Post.objects.select_related('author') for ForeignKey/OneToOne (SQL JOIN). Post.objects.prefetch_related('tags') for ManyToMany/reverse FK (separate query + Python join). Detection: django-debug-toolbar shows duplicate queries. django-silk profiles them. UNSAID: prefetch_related with a custom queryset uses Prefetch() object. Extraction signal: any view doing DB access inside a template loop is likely N+1.

Django signals (pre_save, post_save, post_delete) are synchronous and fire in the same transaction. They're used for side effects: audit logging, cache invalidation, sending emails. UNSAID: signals are invisible coupling — no type checker can trace them. post_save signal calling external APIs will delay saves and fail if the API is down. post_save fires even within transactions that will be rolled back — the signal handler may see data that never ends up in the DB. Best practice: use on_commit() for side effects that must only happen if the transaction commits.

Django migrations track schema changes as Python code. makemigrations generates them; migrate applies them. Migrations must be committed to version control — they ARE the schema history. UNSAID: squashmigrations compresses migration history for large projects. Data migrations (RunPython) mix schema and data changes — dangerous in production if the migration times out or fails halfway. Zero-downtime migration pattern: (1) add nullable column, (2) deploy and backfill, (3) make non-nullable. Extraction signal: migrations with direct SQL (RunSQL) indicate the ORM couldn't express the change.

Django model managers (Manager) encapsulate QuerySet logic. Custom managers add domain-specific query methods: Article.published.all() instead of Article.objects.filter(status='published'). Using get_queryset() override restricts the default queryset globally. UNSAID: overriding get_queryset() in the default manager affects admin, related managers, and all queries — use a separate manager for restricted views. Extraction signal: repeated .filter(is_active=True) or similar boilerplate across views is a sign that a custom manager is needed.

Django uses a single settings.py by convention, but production projects should split settings: base.py (common), local.py (development overrides), production.py (production config). The DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE environment variable selects which to use. SECRET_KEY, DATABASE_URL, and API keys must come from environment variables, never be hardcoded. UNSAID: django-environ or python-decouple are common libraries for reading env vars with type coercion. Extraction signal: SECRET_KEY hardcoded in settings.py is a security vulnerability and indicates the project is not production-ready.

Django REST Framework serializers are the layer between Python objects and JSON. ModelSerializer auto-generates fields from a Model. UNSAID: serializers run validation in two phases — field-level (validate_<fieldname>) then object-level (validate()). read_only_fields prevents clients from overwriting server-set fields (created_by, updated_at). SerializerMethodField for computed fields. The create() and update() methods must be explicitly overridden for nested writable serializers — DRF doesn't auto-handle nested creates. Extraction signal: DRF serializers with nested writable data that don't override create()/update() will silently ignore nested object changes.

DRF ViewSets combine CRUD operations into a single class. ModelViewSet provides list, create, retrieve, update, partial_update, destroy. Routers auto-generate URL patterns from ViewSets. UNSAID: the get_queryset() method is the correct place to filter data by the current user — overriding it ensures every action (list, retrieve, update, delete) is filtered. Not overriding get_queryset() and relying only on object-level permissions leaves list endpoints leaking all users' data. Extraction signal: ViewSets with permission_classes but without user-scoped get_queryset() likely expose cross-user data in list views.

Django's cache framework supports multiple backends: Redis (django-redis), Memcached, filesystem, in-memory. Cache is configured in CACHES setting. cache.set(key, value, timeout), cache.get(key), cache.delete(key). Django's per-view cache (@cache_page) and per-site cache are higher-level. UNSAID: cache invalidation is the hardest part — stale cache causes data inconsistency bugs that are hard to reproduce. Pattern: cache-aside (read from cache, fallback to DB, write to cache). django-cacheops provides ORM-level automatic invalidation. Extraction signal: applications with cache.set() but no cache.delete() on data mutations will serve stale data.

Django's security defaults: CSRF protection (CSRFMiddleware) is on by default for all POST/PUT/DELETE forms. Same-origin cookie policy prevents CSRF. XSS protection via template auto-escaping. Clickjacking protection via X-Frame-Options header. UNSAID: @csrf_exempt is a security downgrade — use it only for webhook endpoints that can't send CSRF tokens (and verify a shared secret instead). Django's DEBUG=True in production disables security headers and exposes tracebacks to browsers — a critical misconfiguration. Extraction signal: DEBUG=True in production settings is a critical security issue that must be caught in deployment checks.

Django + Celery integration: create a celery.py in the project package, call app.config_from_object('django.conf:settings', namespace='CELERY'), and autodiscover tasks with app.autodiscover_tasks(). UNSAID: Django ORM can be used in Celery tasks because Celery workers load Django settings via DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE. But tasks must call django.setup() if run outside the Celery worker. Database connections are NOT shared between Django request threads and Celery workers — each worker has its own connection pool. close_old_connections() should be called at the start of long-running tasks to avoid using stale connections. Extraction signal: Celery tasks that use Django models without proper setup cause 'Apps aren't loaded yet' errors.

Django wraps each request in an atomic transaction by default (ATOMIC_REQUESTS=True). UNSAID: this means a slow request holds a DB transaction open for its entire duration, blocking other queries that need row locks. For requests that do heavy computation and then a final write, use transaction.atomic() only around the write block. Also: exceptions inside @transaction.atomic rollback the transaction — raising an exception in a signal handler rolls back the entire parent transaction. Side effects (emails, external API calls) inside transactions are dangerous because they execute even if the transaction is later rolled back. on_commit() defers code until after successful commit.

Django admin is a powerful auto-generated CRUD interface. Customization points: list_display (columns), list_filter (sidebar filters), search_fields (search bar), readonly_fields, fieldsets (layout). Admin actions (bulk operations). Custom admin views via get_urls(). UNSAID: Django admin is NOT a user-facing product interface — it's a developer/superuser tool. Using admin for customer-facing workflows creates UX, permission, and audit problems. Admin bypasses custom model methods and validation by default — direct DB writes via admin can corrupt application invariants. Extraction signal: business-critical workflows routed through admin are an architectural smell.

Django middleware is a stack of callables that wrap the request/response cycle. New-style middleware: a class with __init__(get_response) and __call__(request). process_view(), process_exception(), process_template_response() hooks for specific phases. UNSAID: middleware order matters — AuthenticationMiddleware must run before SessionMiddleware consumer code, because auth reads from session. Middleware that modifies request.user must run before any middleware or view that reads request.user. Common pattern: RequestID middleware adds a unique ID per request for distributed tracing — must be first in MIDDLEWARE list. Extraction signal: middleware added at the bottom of MIDDLEWARE list that needs to run before auth will silently fail.

Django TestCase wraps each test in a transaction that is rolled back after the test — fast but means signals and on_commit() don't fire. TransactionTestCase truncates tables after each test — slower but tests real transaction behavior. UNSAID: mixing TestCase and TransactionTestCase in the same test suite causes ordering issues. factory_boy is the preferred fixture library over Django fixtures (JSON files) because fixtures are brittle with schema changes. pytest-django provides a django_db mark that's more idiomatic with pytest. Extraction signal: projects that use loaddata for test fixtures will have failing tests after every migration that changes the data shape.

Django Channels extends Django to handle WebSockets, long-polling, and background tasks via ASGI. Channels replaces the WSGI server for async-capable deployments. Channel layers (Redis backend) enable broadcasting to groups of connected clients. UNSAID: Django Channels is NOT async-native for ORM access — sync_to_async() wrapper is needed for database access inside consumers. Running ORM queries directly in async consumers blocks the event loop. Extraction signal: Channels consumers that call ORM methods without sync_to_async will appear to work in tests but will block the ASGI event loop under load.

Django's deployment checklist (manage.py check --deploy) validates: DEBUG=False, SECRET_KEY strength, ALLOWED_HOSTS, HTTPS settings (SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT, HSTS), cookie security (SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE, CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE). UNSAID: ALLOWED_HOSTS=['*'] in production is a Host header injection vulnerability. Static files must be served by the web server or CDN (not Django's development server) — collectstatic gathers them to STATIC_ROOT. WhiteNoise is the simplest solution for serving static files from Django in production without a separate web server. Extraction signal: applications deploying to production without running manage.py check --deploy have unknown security misconfigurations.

Django's permission system: model-level permissions (add, change, delete, view) auto-generated per model. @login_required, @permission_required decorators for views. DRF: permission_classes on ViewSets. Object-level permissions require a custom backend or django-guardian. UNSAID: model-level permissions check if a user CAN perform the action at all; object-level permissions check if they can perform it on SPECIFIC instance. Django admin uses model-level permissions only — users with 'change_user' permission can change ALL users including superusers, a privilege escalation risk. Extraction signal: admin sites where staff users have model-level edit permissions but shouldn't see all records need object-level permissions.

Django 5.x new features: (1) Facet filters in admin — admin.ModelAdmin.show_facets controls per-field facet counts in the sidebar; (2) Field groups in forms — rendered with {% formgroup %} tag for semantic HTML; (3) Database-generated columns — GeneratedField(expression=..., output_field=..., db_persist=True) maps to SQL GENERATED ALWAYS AS columns for computed fields maintained by the DB; (4) LoginRequiredMiddleware — replaces per-view @login_required with a project-wide default, with opt-out @login_not_required for public views. WHY GeneratedField matters: computed columns previously required model properties (Python-only) or custom signals to maintain DB columns. GeneratedField ensures the computation is in the DB and always consistent, even after bulk updates that bypass Python.

Django async views (Django 4.1+): define a view as 'async def my_view(request)' and Django will run it in an async context using ASGI. Async class-based views override async def get()/post(). Django's ORM remains synchronous by default — use sync_to_async() wrapper or the async QuerySet API (Django 4.1+ adds afilter(), aget(), acreate(), acount(), etc.) to avoid blocking the event loop. WHY async views matter: for views that make multiple external API calls, async allows concurrent fetching (asyncio.gather) in a single view, reducing response time from sum-of-calls to max-of-calls. UNSAID: mixing sync ORM calls and async views causes Django to emit a SynchronousOnlyOperation error when running under ASGI — this is intentional to prevent accidental event loop blocking. Extraction signal: Django projects running under ASGI (Daphne, Uvicorn) that use sync ORM calls in async views will fail with SynchronousOnlyOperation.

Django ORM N+1 deep patterns beyond basic select_related: (1) Reverse FK relations — Post.objects.prefetch_related('comments') prefetches all comments in one query; (2) Nested relations — prefetch_related('comments__author') chains prefetches; (3) Annotations instead of properties — annotate(comment_count=Count('comments')) adds a computed field in SQL, avoiding Python-side aggregation loops; (4) Only/defer — .only('id', 'title') fetches fewer columns, avoiding expensive text fields; (5) values_list() for bulk ID extraction without model instantiation overhead. UNSAID: prefetch_related with a filtered Prefetch() object is the only way to apply WHERE conditions to prefetched relations — Post.objects.prefetch_related(Prefetch('comments', queryset=Comment.objects.filter(approved=True))). Using Python filtering after prefetch defeats the purpose (all data is fetched then filtered in Python).

Django migration strategy for zero-downtime deployments: the naive approach (add non-nullable column → deploy) causes downtime because the migration adds the column with NOT NULL before the new code is deployed. Safe three-phase pattern: Phase 1 — add nullable column (no default required, existing rows get NULL), deploy; Phase 2 — backfill with a data migration or management command, add application-level default in code; Phase 3 — add DB-level NOT NULL constraint (fast if all rows filled) and remove nullable. UNSAID: Django's migration framework does NOT automatically detect that removing a field from the model is a breaking change for running instances — the old code reading the dropped column will get AttributeError until fully redeployed. Extraction signal: projects that run migrate before deploying new code can safely add nullable columns; projects that deploy code before migrate can safely read new nullable columns as None.

Django signals anti-patterns: (1) Connecting signals in models.py at module level — signals are connected every time the module is imported, leading to duplicate handler calls if the module is imported multiple times (rare but possible in test setups); correct location is AppConfig.ready(); (2) Signals for business logic — signals create invisible coupling that breaks the 'explicit is better than implicit' principle; business logic in signal handlers is untestable in isolation; (3) post_save for external side effects — the signal fires inside the transaction; if the external call fails, the transaction is already committed; use on_commit() callback to defer external calls until after commit; (4) Circular signal chains — A.post_save triggers B.save() which triggers B.post_save which triggers A.save(). UNSAID: Django doesn't protect against signal recursion — add a 'dispatch_uid' parameter to prevent duplicate registration and guard against circular chains with a flag.

Django REST Framework advanced patterns: (1) Custom authentication — implement BaseAuthentication.authenticate() returning (user, token) tuple or None; (2) Throttling — AnonRateThrottle/UserRateThrottle with DEFAULT_THROTTLE_RATES in settings; custom throttles override get_cache_key(); (3) Filtering — DjangoFilterBackend (django-filter package) for URL query parameter filtering; SearchFilter for full-text; OrderingFilter for sort; (4) Pagination — PageNumberPagination, LimitOffsetPagination, CursorPagination (most scalable — uses opaque cursor, consistent results even with concurrent inserts); (5) Versioning — URLPathVersioning (v1/, v2/) or AcceptHeaderVersioning. UNSAID: CursorPagination requires the queryset to have a stable ordering — without ORDER BY, cursor results are non-deterministic and will return duplicates or skip rows. Extraction signal: DRF list views with LimitOffset pagination on large tables without a DB index on the ordering column will degrade to full table scans.

Django + Celery production integration patterns: (1) Use django-celery-results to store task results in the Django DB — avoids a separate Redis result backend; (2) Use django-celery-beat for periodic tasks stored in DB (editable via admin) instead of static celerybeat_schedule; (3) Task idempotency — Celery guarantees at-least-once delivery; tasks that write to DB must use update_or_create or check existence before creating; (4) Task chaining — chain(fetch_data.s(), process.s(), save.s()) builds a pipeline; chord() for fan-out/fan-in; (5) close_old_connections() at task start prevents using stale DB connections that were created before the worker forked. WHY at-least-once matters: a task can execute twice if the worker crashes after completing but before acknowledging the broker. Idempotent tasks are safe to retry; non-idempotent tasks (send email, charge card) need deduplication logic with a unique task ID stored in the DB.

Django's ORM 'select_related()' uses SQL JOIN and is appropriate for ForeignKey and OneToOne relationships. 'prefetch_related()' uses separate queries and is appropriate for ManyToMany and reverse ForeignKey relationships. Using 'select_related()' on a ManyToMany relationship raises a ValueError or silently falls back to prefetch behavior. Using 'prefetch_related()' on a ForeignKey works but is less efficient than 'select_related()' (two queries vs one JOIN). The classic N+1 problem: 'for book in Book.objects.all(): print(book.author.name)' executes N+1 queries. Fix: 'Book.objects.select_related("author").all()'. Profile with Django Debug Toolbar before assuming you know which queries are slow.

Django's 'F()' expressions enable database-level operations without loading objects into Python. 'Product.objects.filter(id=1).update(stock=F("stock") - 1)' decrements stock atomically in the database, avoiding race conditions that would occur with: 'p = Product.objects.get(id=1); p.stock -= 1; p.save()'. Use 'F()' for: counter increments, comparing fields ('filter(price__gt=F("cost"))'), referencing related model fields in queries ('filter(order__total__gt=F("budget"))'). Combined with 'select_for_update()' for pessimistic locking in concurrent scenarios: 'Product.objects.select_for_update().filter(id=1)'.

Django migrations are generated from model state tracked in migration files, not from the current database schema. Running 'python manage.py makemigrations' when there are unapplied migrations generates a new migration based on the last migration's state, not the current database. If you manually modify the database (add a column, change a type) without creating a corresponding migration, Django won't detect the discrepancy — 'makemigrations --check' won't catch manual DB changes. This causes 'django.db.utils.ProgrammingError' at runtime when the ORM attempts an operation on a column/table that exists in Django's state but not in the actual database or vice versa.

Django's 'Q()' objects enable complex ORM queries with OR, AND, NOT logic. Simple filters chain with implicit AND: 'MyModel.objects.filter(status="active", age__gt=18)'. For OR: 'MyModel.objects.filter(Q(status="active") | Q(status="pending"))'. For NOT: 'MyModel.objects.filter(~Q(status="deleted"))'. Q objects are composable and nestable: 'filter((Q(a=1) | Q(a=2)) & Q(b=True))'. Use Q for dynamic filter building: 'q = Q(); for term in search_terms: q |= Q(title__icontains=term); qs.filter(q)'. Starting with 'Q()' (empty Q) as the identity for OR chains avoids special-casing the first iteration.

Django's 'bulk_create()' does NOT call 'save()' on individual model instances, which means: signals ('post_save', 'pre_save') are NOT fired, custom 'save()' method logic is NOT executed, and 'auto_now_add' fields ARE populated (as a special case). This breaks any business logic relying on signals for side effects (sending emails, updating caches, creating related records). Always audit what 'save()' and 'post_save' signals do before replacing 'save()' calls with 'bulk_create()'. Similarly, 'bulk_update()' does not fire signals. For bulk operations requiring signals, process in batches using 'save()' or fire signals manually after bulk operations.

Django's 'transaction.atomic()' ensures database consistency across multiple ORM operations. Use as context manager or decorator: '@transaction.atomic def create_order(cart): order = Order.objects.create(...); for item in cart.items: OrderItem.objects.create(order=order, ...)'. If any operation inside the atomic block raises an exception, all changes are rolled back. Savepoints enable partial rollbacks: 'with transaction.atomic(): try: with transaction.atomic(): risky_operation(); except: pass; safe_operation()'. Use 'select_for_update()' inside atomic blocks for pessimistic concurrency control. Never open HTTP connections, send emails, or call external APIs inside atomic blocks.

Django signals create hidden coupling that is difficult to debug and test. A 'post_save' signal on User model might update a UserProfile in one module, send a welcome email in another, and invalidate caches in a third — none of these connections are visible from the User model or view code. Failures in signal receivers can cause transaction rollbacks affecting the originating operation. Signal debugging is difficult: set 'DEBUG=True' and use 'Signal.disconnect()' in tests to isolate issues. Prefer explicit service functions over signals for business logic. Use signals only for cross-cutting concerns (audit logging, metrics) where the relationship is truly unidirectional.

Django REST Framework's 'serializers.SerializerMethodField' enables computed fields in API responses. Define the field and a 'get_{field_name}' method: 'class UserSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer): full_name = serializers.SerializerMethodField(); def get_full_name(self, obj): return f"{obj.first_name} {obj.last_name}"'. Method fields are read-only. For writable computed fields, use 'to_representation()' override or a custom field. SerializerMethodField executes for every serialized instance — avoid database queries inside 'get_' methods to prevent N+1. Use 'source' parameter for simple renames: 'email_address = serializers.EmailField(source="email")'.

Django's 'get_or_create()' is NOT atomic in all database backends. Under concurrent requests, two processes can both find that the object doesn't exist and both attempt to create it, causing an IntegrityError on the second create. The correct pattern is: 'try: obj, created = MyModel.objects.get_or_create(field=value); except IntegrityError: obj = MyModel.objects.get(field=value); created = False'. Alternatively, define a unique constraint on the fields used in 'get_or_create' and handle the IntegrityError. This race condition is rare in low-traffic apps but common in high-concurrency systems. 'update_or_create()' has the same issue.

Django's custom model managers enable reusable query logic at the model level. Define: 'class PublishedManager(models.Manager): def get_queryset(self): return super().get_queryset().filter(status="published")'. Attach: 'class Article(models.Model): objects = models.Manager(); published = PublishedManager()'. Use: 'Article.published.all()'. Custom managers with chaining: define methods that return QuerySet for method chaining. Use 'QuerySet' subclass for chainable custom methods: 'class ArticleQuerySet(models.QuerySet): def published(self): return self.filter(status="published"); class Article(models.Model): objects = ArticleQuerySet.as_manager()'. This allows: 'Article.objects.published().filter(date__year=2024)'.

Django's migration squashing combines multiple migrations into one for performance, but squashed migrations require careful management during deployment. A squashed migration depends on ALL migrations it replaces having been applied. If you deploy the squashed migration to a database that hasn't run the original migrations, Django will apply the squashed migration instead. If some but not all original migrations were applied, Django is confused. The safe migration strategy: 1) squash locally, 2) deploy with both original and squashed migrations present, 3) after all servers have applied the squash, remove the original migrations in a subsequent deployment.

Django REST Framework's pagination classes control response size for list endpoints. Use 'PageNumberPagination' for page-number-based ('?page=2'), 'LimitOffsetPagination' for limit/offset ('?limit=20&offset=40'), or 'CursorPagination' for cursor-based (most efficient for large, frequently-changing datasets). Set globally: 'REST_FRAMEWORK = {"DEFAULT_PAGINATION_CLASS": "rest_framework.pagination.PageNumberPagination", "PAGE_SIZE": 20}'. Override per-view with 'pagination_class' attribute. CursorPagination requires an ordering field and prevents page-number jumping, making it ideal for real-time feeds. Always add pagination to list endpoints before going to production.

Django's 'only()' and 'defer()' queryset methods return model instances with some fields not loaded, but accessing a deferred field triggers an additional database query per instance. When iterating over a list and accessing deferred fields, this recreates the N+1 problem you were trying to avoid. Use 'only()' only when you're certain you won't access the excluded fields, or use 'values()' or 'values_list()' for truly flat data access. A subtle failure: serializers accessing model fields that were deferred in the queryset, triggering per-object queries during serialization. Always profile with Django Debug Toolbar after adding 'only()' or 'defer()'.

Django's 'annotate()' adds computed values to each object in a queryset, evaluated in SQL. Essential for avoiding Python-side aggregations: 'Order.objects.annotate(item_count=Count("items"), total=Sum("items__price")).filter(total__gt=100)'. This filters by computed aggregate in SQL — impossible without annotate. Use with 'values()' for GROUP BY: 'Product.objects.values("category").annotate(count=Count("id")).order_by("-count")'. Combine with 'F()' for comparisons between annotated and model fields. 'aggregate()' returns a dict of totals; 'annotate()' adds per-row values. Use 'aggregate()' for grand totals, 'annotate()' for per-row computations.

Django REST Framework's 'perform_create()' and 'perform_update()' are the correct hooks for injecting request-context data (like the authenticated user) into model creation. The common mistake: overriding 'create()' in the view and manually calling 'serializer.save()'. DRF's 'create()' method calls 'perform_create(serializer)' which calls 'serializer.save()'. If you override 'create()' and forget to call 'perform_create()', your overridden 'perform_create()' never runs. Best practice: always override 'perform_create()' to add extra fields: 'def perform_create(self, serializer): serializer.save(author=self.request.user)'. This preserves all of DRF's built-in create logic.

Django's caching framework supports multiple backends and granularities. File-based: simple but slow. Database: auto-setup with migrations. Memcached: fast, production-grade, multi-server. Redis: most feature-rich, supports pub/sub, sets, sorted sets beyond simple caching. Configure: 'CACHES = {"default": {"BACKEND": "django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache", "LOCATION": "redis://127.0.0.1:6379"}}'. View-level: '@cache_page(60 * 15)'. Template fragment: '{% cache 500 sidebar request.user.id %}'. Low-level API: 'cache.set(key, value, timeout)'. Use 'cache_page' sparingly on views with user-specific content — cache per-user with key prefix or use low-level API.

Django's migration dependency system means migration files should never be deleted or modified after being applied in any environment. Deleting an applied migration file causes 'django.db.migrations.exceptions.NodeNotFoundInGraph' errors and prevents running 'migrate'. If you need to clean up many small migrations, use squashmigrations — never delete migration files directly. A common failure during development: deleting all migrations and regenerating them ('python manage.py makemigrations') which breaks any deployed environments that have the original migrations in their 'django_migrations' table. Use 'migrate --fake' to repair state when absolutely necessary.

Django's class-based views (CBVs) use method resolution order (MRO) for mixing multiple view behaviors via mixins. 'LoginRequiredMixin' must be listed before the view class: 'class MyView(LoginRequiredMixin, DetailView)'. Order matters because Python's MRO processes left to right. Commonly used DRF mixins: 'CreateModelMixin', 'RetrieveModelMixin', 'UpdateModelMixin', 'DestroyModelMixin', 'ListModelMixin'. Combine with 'GenericAPIView' for custom endpoint combinations without the full CRUD overhead. For complex views, prefer function-based views or explicit method implementations over deep mixin hierarchies that obscure the actual behavior.

Django's 'settings.DEBUG = True' in production causes several security issues: detailed error pages with code excerpts and local variables (potentially exposing secrets), 'django.test.utils.override_settings' affecting security behavior, and performance degradation from keeping all SQL queries in memory ('connection.queries'). More subtly, 'DEBUG=True' makes Django serve static files directly (bypassing whitenoise or nginx) and disables security middleware checks. Always verify 'DEBUG=False' in production. Use environment variable: 'DEBUG = os.environ.get("DEBUG", "False") == "True"' — note the string comparison since env vars are always strings.

Django REST Framework's 'ViewSet' and 'Router' system automates URL patterns for standard CRUD operations. Register: 'router = DefaultRouter(); router.register(r"users", UserViewSet)'. This generates: GET/POST /users/, GET/PUT/PATCH/DELETE /users/{pk}/. 'ModelViewSet' provides all CRUD; use 'ReadOnlyModelViewSet' for read-only APIs. For custom actions, use '@action' decorator: '@action(detail=True, methods=["post"]) def activate(self, request, pk=None)' generates POST /users/{pk}/activate/. Override 'get_queryset()' for filtering and 'get_serializer_class()' for context-dependent serializers. Use 'get_permissions()' and 'get_throttle_classes()' for per-action permission control.

Django's custom template tags and filters extend template functionality for reusable presentation logic. Register a template library: 'from django import template; register = template.Library()'. Simple filter: '@register.filter def truncate_words(value, num): return " ".join(value.split()[:num])'. Use: '{{ article.body|truncate_words:50 }}'. Inclusion tag for reusable HTML components: '@register.inclusion_tag("partials/card.html") def render_card(item): return {"item": item}'. Load: '{% load mytags %}{% render_card post %}'. Use 'simple_tag' for tags returning a value, 'inclusion_tag' for rendering template fragments. Keep template tags thin — complex logic belongs in models or services.

Django's 'save()' method, when called without 'update_fields', performs a full UPDATE of all model fields. In a concurrent environment, two processes reading and saving the same model will cause one to overwrite the other's changes (last-write-wins). Solution for partial updates: use 'instance.save(update_fields=["status", "updated_at"])' to update only the modified fields. Solution for concurrent modifications: use 'select_for_update()' to lock the row, or use 'F()' expressions for atomic increments. Never use 'obj.field += 1; obj.save()' for counters — always use 'MyModel.objects.filter(pk=obj.pk).update(field=F("field") + 1)'.

Django REST Framework's 'permissions.py' system enables fine-grained access control. Built-in permissions: 'IsAuthenticated', 'IsAdminUser', 'IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly'. Custom: 'class IsOwner(BasePermission): def has_object_permission(self, request, view, obj): return obj.owner == request.user'. Compose: 'permission_classes = [IsAuthenticated & IsOwner]' (both required) or '[IsAuthenticated | IsAdminUser]' (either). 'has_permission' runs on every request; 'has_object_permission' runs only when the view retrieves a specific object. Always implement both for object-level permissions. Set globally: 'REST_FRAMEWORK = {"DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES": ["rest_framework.permissions.IsAuthenticated"]}' and override per-view.

Django's 'ALLOWED_HOSTS' setting is enforced only when 'DEBUG=False'. In development with DEBUG=True, any host is allowed. This means developers never see host-validation failures locally, then are surprised when they deploy. ALLOWED_HOSTS must include the production domain, load balancer hostname, and internal service hostnames (for health checks). Forgetting to add a subdomain causes 'DisallowedHost: Invalid HTTP_HOST header' in production. Use 'ALLOWED_HOSTS = os.environ.get("ALLOWED_HOSTS", "").split(",")' to configure from environment variables. Add '*' only for trusted internal services that don't face the internet.

Django's 'abstract base classes' in models enable shared fields and behavior without database-level inheritance. 'class TimeStampedModel(models.Model): created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True); updated_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True); class Meta: abstract = True'. All models inheriting from TimeStampedModel get these fields in their own tables (no JOIN needed). Contrast with multi-table inheritance (no 'abstract=True') which creates a separate table per class level with automatic JOINs — expensive for large datasets. Use abstract base classes for shared fields; use multi-table inheritance sparingly only when the IS-A relationship and polymorphic querying justify the JOIN cost.

Django's 'ManyToManyField' with a 'through' model enables extra data on the relationship. Without 'through', Django auto-creates a join table and manages it. When you later add a 'through' model, you must manually migrate from the auto-created table — there's no automated migration path. Plan your data model upfront: if you might need relationship metadata (join date, role, status), add the 'through' model from the start even if it's currently empty. Adding 'through' retroactively requires a complex migration: create new through table, copy data from old join table, update the ManyToManyField to use 'through'.

Django REST Framework's 'throttling' controls request rate to prevent abuse. Built-in throttles: 'AnonRateThrottle' (by IP for unauthenticated), 'UserRateThrottle' (by user for authenticated). Configure: 'REST_FRAMEWORK = {"DEFAULT_THROTTLE_CLASSES": ["rest_framework.throttling.AnonRateThrottle"], "DEFAULT_THROTTLE_RATES": {"anon": "100/day", "user": "1000/day"}}'. Custom scoped throttle: 'class LoginRateThrottle(ScopedRateThrottle): scope = "login"' with rate '"login": "5/hour"'. Override 'get_cache_key()' for custom throttle keys (e.g., per-API-key). Use Redis-backed cache backend for throttle state across multiple workers.

Django's 'ContentType' framework and 'GenericForeignKey' enable polymorphic relationships but have severe performance implications. Every access to a generic foreign key requires: 1) a query to the 'django_content_types' table, 2) a query to the target model's table. For lists of objects with generic foreign keys, use 'prefetch_related' with 'GenericRelatedObjectManager.prefetch_related_objects()'. A common anti-pattern: using GenericForeignKey for simple use cases where a regular ForeignKey or Union type would work better. GenericForeignKey also prevents database-level foreign key constraints and cascades, making referential integrity the application's responsibility.

Django's 'Celery' integration for background task processing follows established patterns. Use 'django-celery-results' for storing task results in Django database. Define tasks in each app's 'tasks.py': '@shared_task(bind=True, max_retries=3) def send_email(self, user_id): try: user = User.objects.get(pk=user_id); send_email_to(user); except Exception as exc: raise self.retry(exc=exc, countdown=60)'. Use 'bind=True' for access to 'self' (the task instance) for retries. 'countdown' specifies retry delay in seconds. Configure periodic tasks with 'django-celery-beat' using a database schedule. Always use 'on_commit' hook to dispatch tasks: 'transaction.on_commit(lambda: my_task.delay(obj.pk))' — prevents tasks from running before the transaction commits.

Django's 'on_commit' callbacks are tied to the outermost atomic transaction. When called inside a nested 'transaction.atomic()' (savepoint), the callback doesn't fire until the outermost transaction commits. If the outermost transaction rolls back (even though the inner savepoint committed successfully from the inner block's perspective), the on_commit callback never fires. This means if you rely on 'on_commit' to dispatch Celery tasks and the outer transaction rolls back, tasks are never sent — which is the correct behavior. Developers sometimes expect callbacks from savepoints to fire when the savepoint commits; they don't.

Django's 'custom User model' must be defined before your first migration. Django's built-in User model is the hardest thing to change after project start — swapping it after migrations exist requires resetting all migrations. Always define a custom User model even if you don't need extra fields yet: 'class User(AbstractUser): pass' in an 'accounts' app, then 'AUTH_USER_MODEL = "accounts.User"'. This future-proofs the model for additions. For completely custom auth (no username, use email instead): extend 'AbstractBaseUser' and define a custom 'UserManager'. Never import User directly — use 'get_user_model()' throughout your code to respect 'AUTH_USER_MODEL'.

Django REST Framework's 'ModelSerializer' 'depth' option enables nested serialization of related objects, but it's dangerous in production APIs. 'depth = 2' serializes all fields of all related models up to 2 levels deep, including sensitive fields you might not want exposed. Users of a serializer with depth don't control what gets serialized — adding a field to a related model automatically exposes it through all serializers using depth. Always use explicit nested serializers: 'author = AuthorSerializer(read_only=True)' where 'AuthorSerializer' explicitly declares only the fields you want exposed. Never use 'depth' in production serializers.

Django's database routing enables directing reads and writes to different databases. Define a router class: 'class PrimaryReplicaRouter: def db_for_read(self, model, **hints): return "replica"; def db_for_write(self, model, **hints): return "default"; def allow_relation(self, obj1, obj2, **hints): return True; def allow_migrate(self, db, app_label, **hints): return db == "default"'. Configure: 'DATABASE_ROUTERS = ["myapp.routers.PrimaryReplicaRouter"]' and define multiple databases in 'DATABASES'. The replica database should be read-only at DB level too. Use 'using("default")' on querysets that must read from primary to avoid replication lag issues.

Django's 'ImageField' and 'FileField' store only the file path in the database, not the actual file. Deleting a model instance with these fields does NOT delete the associated files from storage — orphaned files accumulate indefinitely. Implement cleanup via 'post_delete' signal: '@receiver(post_delete, sender=MyModel) def delete_file(sender, instance, **kwargs): instance.image.delete(save=False)'. Similarly, replacing an image (saving with a new file) leaves the old file on disk unless you implement cleanup in 'pre_save' or 'save()'. Use 'django-cleanup' library for automatic file deletion on model delete/replace.

Django REST Framework's custom authentication enables token-based, JWT, or API-key authentication. Implement 'BaseAuthentication' subclass: 'class APIKeyAuthentication(BaseAuthentication): def authenticate(self, request): api_key = request.META.get("HTTP_X_API_KEY"); if not api_key: return None; try: key = APIKey.objects.get(key=api_key, active=True); return (key.user, key); except APIKey.DoesNotExist: raise AuthenticationFailed("Invalid API key")'. Return 'None' (not an exception) when the auth scheme's credentials are absent — this allows other authenticators to try. Raise 'AuthenticationFailed' when credentials are present but invalid.

Django's 'auto_now' and 'auto_now_add' on DateTimeField/DateField cannot be overridden by passing the value in model creation or save. 'auto_now_add=True' always uses the current time at creation; 'auto_now=True' always uses current time on every save. This makes it impossible to set these fields to custom values, which is a problem for data imports, fixtures, and testing. Use 'default=timezone.now' instead of 'auto_now_add=True' for fields you might need to control, with 'editable=False, blank=True' to prevent form/serializer exposure. 'auto_now' fields are even more problematic — use 'F("updated_at")' expressions or custom save logic for explicit control.

Django's 'django-storages' with S3 or GCS enables cloud file storage with minimal code changes. Install 'django-storages[s3]' and configure: 'DEFAULT_FILE_STORAGE = "storages.backends.s3boto3.S3Boto3Storage"; AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = "mybucket"; AWS_S3_REGION_NAME = "us-east-1"'. For static files: 'STATICFILES_STORAGE = "storages.backends.s3boto3.S3StaticStorage"'. Use separate storage classes for public vs private files: public with 'AWS_DEFAULT_ACL = "public-read"', private with ACL None and signed URLs via 'instance.file.url' (generates presigned URL). For large uploads, use 'AWS_S3_MAX_MEMORY_SIZE' and multipart upload configuration.

Django's admin site automatically discovers and registers models from 'admin.py' files in each app, but only when the app is in 'INSTALLED_APPS'. A common deployment failure: an app that works locally fails in production because its admin.py imports a model from another app that hasn't been loaded yet, causing 'AppRegistryNotReady' exception. Fix: use lazy model imports inside functions rather than at module level, or ensure proper INSTALLED_APPS ordering. Another failure: registering the same model twice with different admin classes causes 'AlreadyRegistered' error — use 'admin.site.unregister(Model)' before re-registering.

Django's 'django-filter' library integrates with DRF for flexible queryset filtering. Define: 'class ProductFilter(django_filters.FilterSet): min_price = django_filters.NumberFilter(field_name="price", lookup_expr="gte"); max_price = django_filters.NumberFilter(field_name="price", lookup_expr="lte"); class Meta: model = Product; fields = ["category", "in_stock"]'. Use in ViewSet: 'filter_backends = [DjangoFilterBackend, SearchFilter, OrderingFilter]; filterset_class = ProductFilter; search_fields = ["name", "description"]; ordering_fields = ["price", "created_at"]'. This generates filterable, searchable, orderable APIs without custom view code.

Django's 'middleware' order in MIDDLEWARE setting is critical and errors are silent. Request processing goes top-to-bottom through middleware; response processing goes bottom-to-top. 'SecurityMiddleware' must be first to enforce HTTPS redirects before other middleware processes potentially insecure requests. 'SessionMiddleware' must come before 'AuthenticationMiddleware' since auth depends on session. Placing 'CommonMiddleware' before 'SessionMiddleware' causes session data loss on trailing-slash redirects. Django's middleware order in the default project template is carefully tuned — always add custom middleware after researching the correct position, not at the end by default.

Django's 'Prefetch()' object enables fine-grained prefetch control: filtering prefetched results and using different querysets. 'User.objects.prefetch_related(Prefetch("orders", queryset=Order.objects.filter(status="active").select_related("product"), to_attr="active_orders"))'. The 'to_attr' parameter stores results as a list attribute (not a manager), enabling access without additional queries. Combine multiple Prefetch objects: 'prefetch_related(Prefetch("orders"), Prefetch("reviews"))'. For M2M with through model: 'prefetch_related(Prefetch("memberships", queryset=Membership.objects.select_related("group")))'. Nested prefetches work: 'Prefetch("orders", queryset=Order.objects.prefetch_related("items"))'.

Django's CSRF protection is bypassed by incorrectly excluding views with '@csrf_exempt'. This decorator disables ALL CSRF protection for that view. A common failure: applying '@csrf_exempt' to fix webhook endpoints and accidentally excluding API endpoints that should be protected. For webhooks with signature-based authentication, '@csrf_exempt' is correct — but restrict to specific paths. For DRF views, CSRF is handled by the authentication classes (session auth requires CSRF, token auth doesn't) — adding '@csrf_exempt' to DRF views is usually unnecessary and potentially dangerous. Use 'CsrfExemptSessionAuthentication' in DRF for session-based auth without CSRF (non-standard; document why).

Django's 'django-environ' or 'python-decouple' enables clean environment-based configuration. With python-decouple: 'from decouple import config; SECRET_KEY = config("SECRET_KEY"); DEBUG = config("DEBUG", default=False, cast=bool); DATABASE_URL = config("DATABASE_URL")'. Load from '.env' file in development, environment variables in production (env vars take precedence). Use 'dj-database-url' to parse DATABASE_URL strings: 'DATABASES = {"default": dj_database_url.parse(DATABASE_URL)}'. This single-line configuration handles all database types (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite) from a single URL string. Commit '.env.example' with placeholder values; never commit '.env'.

Django's 'QuerySet.iterator()' bypasses the in-memory cache and streams results directly, ideal for processing large datasets without memory exhaustion. However, 'iterator()' is incompatible with 'prefetch_related()' — prefetching requires loading all objects into memory to build the prefetch cache. Using 'iterator()' with 'prefetch_related()' silently disables prefetching, potentially causing N+1 queries. Also, 'iterator()' doesn't work with sliced querysets. For large datasets with related objects, process in batches: 'qs.order_by("pk")' then use keyset pagination (WHERE pk > last_pk LIMIT 1000) rather than 'iterator()' with 'prefetch_related()'.

Django's 'django-debug-toolbar' is essential for local performance profiling. It shows: SQL queries with count, time, and duplicate detection; template rendering tree with timing; cache operations; signal receivers fired per request. Install: add to INSTALLED_APPS and MIDDLEWARE (after other middleware). Configure: 'INTERNAL_IPS = ["127.0.0.1"]'. The 'SQLPanel' is the most valuable — identify N+1 queries before they reach production. Use 'debug_toolbar.panels.profiling.ProfilingPanel' for function-level profiling. In tests, 'override_settings(DEBUG_TOOLBAR_PANELS=[])' to disable for speed. Never install in production.

Django's 'DateTimeField(default=datetime.now)' vs 'default=datetime.now()' is a critical distinction. Using 'default=datetime.now()' (with parentheses) evaluates the datetime once at class definition time — all records will have the same creation timestamp (the time the Python module was imported). Using 'default=datetime.now' (without parentheses) evaluates at instance creation time. This is a common Python gotcha that affects any mutable default: lists, dicts, dates. The correct pattern is always 'default=timezone.now' (without parens) or 'auto_now_add=True'. Django's system checks may not catch this bug; it manifests as suspicious data with identical timestamps.

Django REST Framework's 'filtering with custom lookup fields' enables powerful search capabilities. Use 'SearchFilter': 'search_fields = ["^name", "=email", "@bio", "name"]' where '^' means starts-with, '=' means exact match, '@' means full-text search (database-specific), and no prefix means icontains. For DRF's 'OrderingFilter': 'ordering_fields = ["created_at", "price"]; ordering = ["-created_at"]'. Combine with annotation for sorting by computed values: override 'get_queryset()' to annotate, then add the annotated field to 'ordering_fields'. For complex filtering logic that exceeds FilterSet capabilities, override 'filter_queryset()' on the ViewSet.

Django's test runner creates a test database by default and runs each test in a transaction that's rolled back after the test. 'TestCase' (from 'django.test') wraps each test in a transaction. 'TransactionTestCase' (base class) doesn't — it resets the database between tests by truncating tables, which is slower. Tests that test signals, 'on_commit' callbacks, or database-level constraints must use 'TransactionTestCase' because these behaviors require transaction commit. A subtle failure: 'on_commit' callbacks tested with regular 'TestCase' never fire because the transaction never commits. Use 'TestCase.captureOnCommitCallbacks()' context manager (Django 4.1+) to test on_commit callbacks with TestCase.

Django's 'Channels' library extends Django to handle WebSockets, long-polling, and other async protocols using ASGI. Define a consumer: 'class ChatConsumer(AsyncWebsocketConsumer): async def connect(self): await self.channel_layer.group_add(self.room_name, self.channel_name); await self.accept()'. Use Django Channels' layer (backed by Redis) for group messaging: 'await self.channel_layer.group_send(room_name, {"type": "chat.message", "message": text})'. Configure routing in 'asgi.py'. Run with Daphne or Uvicorn as ASGI server. Use the channel layer for broadcasting from Django views to WebSocket clients: 'channel_layer.group_send()' from synchronous code using 'async_to_sync()'.

Django REST Framework's 'write_only=True' on a serializer field prevents the field from being included in serialized output (responses) while allowing it in input (requests). A common failure: defining 'password = serializers.CharField(write_only=True)' but forgetting that 'ModelSerializer' might add the field with 'write_only=False' if 'password' is in the model's fields list. Always verify write_only behavior by testing the actual API response. DRF's extra_kwargs provides centralized write_only declaration: 'class Meta: extra_kwargs = {"password": {"write_only": True}}'. Use this consistently rather than scattered field declarations.

Django's 'values()' and 'values_list()' return dictionaries or tuples instead of model instances, significantly reducing memory usage and query time for read-only data access. 'User.objects.values("id", "email").filter(active=True)' returns dicts. 'values_list("id", flat=True)' returns a flat list of IDs. Use for: data exports, API responses where you control serialization, and bulk operations on IDs. 'values_list("created_at", flat=True)' for stats and aggregations. Limitation: no model methods or properties available on dict/tuple results. For complex operations, use annotation with values: 'values("category").annotate(count=Count("id"))' for GROUP BY aggregations.

Django's admin 'list_display' cannot include ManyToMany fields directly — it raises an 'ImproperlyConfigured' error. Use a custom method on the ModelAdmin class: 'def get_categories(self, obj): return ", ".join(c.name for c in obj.categories.all())'. This triggers N+1 queries in the admin list view. Fix with 'prefetch_related': override 'get_queryset()' on the ModelAdmin to prefetch: 'def get_queryset(self, request): return super().get_queryset(request).prefetch_related("categories")'. The admin panel is a common source of unnoticed N+1 queries — monitor it with django-debug-toolbar even if the public API is optimized.

Django's 'CheckConstraint' and 'UniqueConstraint' in model Meta enforce business rules at the database level. 'class Meta: constraints = [models.CheckConstraint(check=Q(price__gte=0), name="price_non_negative"), models.UniqueConstraint(fields=["user", "product"], name="unique_user_product_review")]'. Database-level constraints catch violations that application-level validation misses (direct SQL inserts, ORM bypasses, concurrent insertions). 'UniqueConstraint' with 'condition' parameter enables partial unique indexes: 'UniqueConstraint(fields=["email"], condition=Q(active=True), name="unique_active_email")'. Constraints generate migrations and are enforced by the database engine.

Django's management commands run in the context of the Django application with DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE set. A common failure: using relative imports or assuming a working directory in management commands, which breaks when run via cron or supervisord from different directories. Always use absolute paths: 'BASE_DIR = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent.parent'. Another failure: management commands that call 'sys.exit()' directly — management commands should raise 'CommandError' for errors instead. Calling 'sys.exit()' inside a management command called from another context (tests, other commands) terminates the entire process unexpectedly.

Django's 'custom validators' at the field level reuse validation logic across models and forms. Define: 'def validate_no_profanity(value): if any(word in value.lower() for word in PROFANITY_LIST): raise ValidationError("Content contains prohibited words")'. Apply to model field: 'bio = models.TextField(validators=[validate_no_profanity, MaxLengthValidator(500)])'. Validators run when 'full_clean()' is called (including in forms and DRF serializers) but NOT automatically on 'save()'. To enforce validators on save: override 'save()' to call 'self.full_clean()'. DRF serializers run validators on 'is_valid()'. Share validators between model and serializer to maintain single source of truth.

Django REST Framework's 'HyperlinkedModelSerializer' and 'HyperlinkedRelatedField' generate URLs as relationship identifiers instead of PKs. They require the view to be named consistently: a 'User' model needs a 'user-detail' view name by default. Renaming views without updating serializers breaks URL generation with 'NoReverseMatch' errors. DRF's URL-based serializers also require the request context to generate absolute URLs: serializers used outside views (in Celery tasks, management commands) need explicit request injection. Custom 'view_name' parameter overrides the default naming: 'HyperlinkedRelatedField(view_name="api:user-detail", read_only=True)'.

Django's 'async views' (Django 4.1+) enable non-blocking request handling. Define with 'async def': 'async def my_view(request): data = await fetch_external_api(); return JsonResponse(data)'. Async views run on Uvicorn/Daphne (ASGI servers) but also work on gunicorn with async worker class. Existing Django ORM is synchronous — wrap ORM calls with 'sync_to_async': 'user = await sync_to_async(User.objects.get)(pk=pk)'. Or use 'database_sync_to_async' decorator from channels. Django's async tests use 'AsyncClient': 'from django.test import AsyncClient'. Caveat: mixing sync and async code requires careful handling of thread affinity; avoid calling sync Django functions from async context without wrapping.

Django's 'unique_together' Meta option is deprecated in favor of 'UniqueConstraint' in Meta.constraints. Using both on the same fields creates duplicate constraints in the database. The migration system treats them separately, so you end up with two constraints where one is sufficient. Worse, removing 'unique_together' generates a migration that drops the constraint, and removing 'UniqueConstraint' generates another — if you do both simultaneously in one migration, the final state may have no constraint. Always migrate incrementally: add UniqueConstraint first, deploy, then remove unique_together in a separate migration, verify, then deploy again.

Django's 'django-silk' enables query and request profiling in development/staging. It records every request with query count, query time, and code paths. Unlike Debug Toolbar (per-request in browser), Silk stores history and enables analysis of slow endpoints over time. Configure: add 'silk' to INSTALLED_APPS and MIDDLEWARE; run 'manage.py migrate' for Silk tables; access '/silk/' for the UI. Use '@silk_profile(name="MyFunction")' decorator to profile specific functions. For production profiling, Silk can be conditionally enabled: 'SILKY_PYTHON_PROFILER = True' for Python-level profiling. Clear Silk data regularly with 'manage.py silk_clear_request_log'.

Django REST Framework's 'to_representation()' override runs on serialization (output) while 'to_internal_value()' runs on deserialization (input). A common mistake: overriding 'to_representation()' to transform output data while inadvertently breaking input handling, or vice versa. Each method has different responsibilities: 'to_internal_value()' validates and converts input data to Python objects; 'to_representation()' converts model instances to output dicts. When overriding both, ensure field name changes are consistent. The 'validate()' method (cross-field validation) runs after 'to_internal_value()' — use for business rule validation that requires multiple fields.

Django's 'django-health-check' library provides a pluggable health check framework for production readiness. Install and add to INSTALLED_APPS; configure checks: database, cache, storage backends, Celery worker availability. The '/ht/' endpoint returns 200 if all checks pass, 500 if any fail. Custom check: 'class MyServiceCheck(BaseHealthCheckBackend): def check_status(self): if not external_service_available(): self.add_error(ServiceUnavailable("Service unreachable"))'. Register: 'from health_check.plugins import plugin_dir; plugin_dir.register(MyServiceCheck)'. Use separate liveness (process alive) and readiness (can serve traffic) endpoints for Kubernetes probe configuration.

Django's 'ATOMIC_REQUESTS = True' database setting wraps every HTTP request in a transaction, ensuring atomicity but creating long-lived transactions that hold database locks. Long requests (file uploads, calls to slow external APIs) keep transactions open, potentially blocking other requests waiting for locks on the same rows. Solutions: use 'transaction.non_atomic_requests()' decorator on views that shouldn't be wrapped, or process the slow parts outside the request transaction (Celery tasks). Also, never send emails, call external APIs, or publish to message queues inside request-level transactions — if the email succeeds but the DB transaction rolls back, you have inconsistent state.

Django's 'fulltext search' with PostgreSQL uses 'SearchVector', 'SearchQuery', and 'SearchRank' from 'django.contrib.postgres'. Basic: 'Entry.objects.filter(content__search="django tutorial")'. Advanced with ranking: 'qs = Entry.objects.annotate(rank=SearchRank(SearchVector("title", weight="A") + SearchVector("body", weight="B"), SearchQuery(query))).filter(rank__gte=0.1).order_by("-rank")'. Create a 'SearchVectorField' with a database trigger for pre-computed vectors on large tables. For language-specific stemming: 'SearchQuery(query, config="english")'. For small datasets, 'trigram similarity' (pg_trgm) handles typos better than stemmed search.

Django's 'ForeignKey' with 'on_delete=models.CASCADE' deletes child objects when the parent is deleted. With 'on_delete=models.PROTECT', deletion is blocked if children exist. Choosing the wrong option has severe consequences: CASCADE can delete thousands of records from a single delete operation; PROTECT surfaces as an IntegrityError that developers must handle explicitly. 'SET_NULL' and 'SET_DEFAULT' are safer for optional relationships. A common production failure: deleting a User with CASCADE set on profile, orders, reviews, and everything else — a single admin action cascades to delete gigabytes of data. Audit every CASCADE on models that represent important user data.

Django's 'class-based views' with 'get_context_data()' override enables adding data to template context without reimplementing 'get()': 'class ArticleDetailView(DetailView): model = Article; def get_context_data(self, **kwargs): ctx = super().get_context_data(**kwargs); ctx["related"] = Article.objects.filter(category=self.object.category).exclude(pk=self.object.pk)[:5]; return ctx'. Always call 'super().get_context_data()' and update the returned dict. For context shared across all views, use context processors: 'TEMPLATES[0]["OPTIONS"]["context_processors"]' + custom processor function that returns a dict. Context processors run on every request — keep them lightweight.

Django REST Framework's 'perform_destroy()' (called by DestroyModelMixin) deletes objects without any soft-delete behavior. Many production applications need 'deleted_at' timestamp instead of actual deletion for audit trails. Implementing soft-delete: override 'perform_destroy()' to set 'deleted_at' field; override 'get_queryset()' to exclude soft-deleted objects with '.filter(deleted_at__isnull=True)'. Use a custom manager to always exclude deleted records. Danger: if you add soft-delete after initial deployment, existing code doing '.all()' or '.filter()' without the custom manager will include deleted records. Use 'django-safedelete' library for a consistent soft-delete implementation.

Django's 'index_together' and 'Meta.indexes' control database indexes for query performance. Single-column: 'models.Index(fields=["email"])'. Composite: 'models.Index(fields=["last_name", "first_name"])'. Partial index (PostgreSQL): 'models.Index(fields=["status"], condition=Q(status="active"), name="active_status_idx")'. Functional index: 'models.Index(Upper("email"), name="email_upper_idx")'. Indexes cost write performance and storage; add only when you have query evidence. Use 'EXPLAIN ANALYZE' (PostgreSQL) to verify index usage. Add indexes via migrations — never directly on the database without a corresponding migration file.

Django's 'request.user' is populated by 'AuthenticationMiddleware', which must run after 'SessionMiddleware'. If middleware is misconfigured, 'request.user' is 'AnonymousUser' even for authenticated users. Similarly, DRF's authentication runs separately from Django's session authentication — 'request.user' in DRF is set by DRF's authentication classes, not Django's middleware. A view using DRF's 'APIView' has DRF auth; a plain Django view uses Django's session auth. Mixing both in the same project can cause confusion where 'request.user' behaves differently depending on which view class is used. Document this dual-auth system clearly.

Django's 'django-celery-beat' with database-backed periodic tasks enables dynamic scheduling without restarting workers. Create periodic tasks via admin or code: 'PeriodicTask.objects.create(crontab=CrontabSchedule.objects.get_or_create(minute=0, hour=6)[0], task="myapp.tasks.daily_report", name="Daily Report")'. Tasks store their schedule in the database, enabling modification at runtime. Use 'IntervalSchedule' for fixed intervals, 'CrontabSchedule' for cron-style schedules. Celery Beat periodically checks the database for task schedule changes (configurable with 'CELERY_BEAT_SCHEDULER'). In production, run exactly one Beat process — multiple Beat processes cause duplicate task scheduling.

Django's 'TestCase.setUpTestData()' runs once per class and shares data across all test methods in the class via a transaction savepoint. Modifying the shared data in a test method corrupts it for subsequent tests in the same class. Objects created in 'setUpTestData' are wrapped in a transaction; mutations are rolled back between tests only if they go through the database (ORM operations are rolled back, but in-memory modifications to model instances are NOT rolled back). Always create fresh data or call 'refresh_from_db()' in tests that modify shared objects. For truly isolated tests, move to 'setUp()' which runs per-test.

Django's 'OpenAPI schema generation' with drf-spectacular produces accurate OpenAPI 3.0 specs. Install 'drf-spectacular' and configure: 'REST_FRAMEWORK = {"DEFAULT_SCHEMA_CLASS": "drf_spectacular.openapi.AutoSchema"}'. Generate schema: 'manage.py spectacular --file schema.yml'. Add to URLs for live docs: 'path("api/schema/", SpectacularAPIView.as_view()), path("api/docs/", SpectacularSwaggerView.as_view(url_name="schema"))'. Extend schema hints with '@extend_schema' decorator: '@extend_schema(responses=UserSerializer, parameters=[...])'. Use 'SpectacularJSONAPIView' for JSON format. Commit the generated schema to version control and add CI check to detect undocumented API changes.

Django's 'model inheritance' with proxy models shares the database table but allows different Python behavior (custom methods, managers, Meta options). A subtle failure: creating a proxy model and adding a custom default manager that filters results — users of the proxy model see filtered results, users of the parent model see all results, both operating on the same table. This can cause confusion and subtle bugs when code inadvertently uses the parent model instead of the proxy. Document clearly which model to use in which context. Proxy model managers don't affect the parent model's querysets.

Django's 'django-extensions' library provides useful development utilities: 'shell_plus' auto-imports all models; 'runserver_plus' uses Werkzeug's debugger; 'graph_models' generates ER diagrams; 'show_urls' lists all URL patterns. 'shell_plus --print-sql' shows all SQL queries during interactive session. 'sqldiff' shows differences between model definitions and actual database schema. 'validate_templates' checks templates for syntax errors without running the server. In CI, 'validate_templates' can catch template errors that would only manifest at runtime. Use 'graph_models -a -g -o my_project_visualized.png' to generate ER diagrams for documentation.

Django's 'static files' with 'collectstatic' copies files from each app's 'static/' directory to 'STATIC_ROOT'. If two apps have files with the same path (e.g., both have 'static/css/style.css'), the last app processed wins — silently overwriting the other's file. This causes mysterious UI breakage where one app's styles override another's. Fix: namespace static files within app subdirectories: 'myapp/static/myapp/css/style.css' and reference as 'myapp/css/style.css'. Use 'STATICFILES_DIRS' for project-level static files that don't belong to any app. Run 'manage.py findstatic <filename>' to identify which app's file would win.

Django's 'factory_boy' with 'DjangoModelFactory' enables clean test data setup with complex relationships. Define: 'class UserFactory(DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = User; username = factory.Sequence(lambda n: f"user{n}"); email = factory.LazyAttribute(lambda obj: f"{obj.username}@example.com")'. Nested factories: 'class PostFactory(DjangoModelFactory): author = factory.SubFactory(UserFactory)'. Creating with overrides: 'PostFactory(author=specific_user, title="Custom Title")'. Use 'factory.CREATE_STRATEGY' for batch creation. 'factory.build()' creates without saving (for unit tests). Trait-based variations: '@factory.post_generation def with_comments(self, create, extracted, **kwargs)'. Prefer factory_boy over hand-coded 'setUp()' for maintainable test data.

Django's 'JSONField' (added in Django 3.1) stores JSON data natively but has cross-database compatibility issues. 'JSONField' on SQLite uses TEXT; on PostgreSQL uses JSONB (queryable); on MySQL uses JSON type. Queries like 'filter(data__key="value")' work across all backends but 'filter(data__contains={...})' and JSON path lookups are PostgreSQL-specific. A common failure: developing with SQLite where JSON queries silently fall back to string matching, then deploying to PostgreSQL where behavior differs. Test with the target database backend (or use 'django.test.utils.override_settings' with PostgreSQL in CI even if SQLite in development).

Django REST Framework's 'versioning' enables maintaining multiple API versions. URL-based: 'class MyView(APIView): versioning_class = URLPathVersioning'. Configure: 'REST_FRAMEWORK = {"DEFAULT_VERSIONING_CLASS": "rest_framework.versioning.NamespaceVersioning", "ALLOWED_VERSIONS": ["v1", "v2"]}'. Access version: 'request.version'. Use different serializers per version: 'def get_serializer_class(self): if self.request.version == "v1": return UserSerializerV1; return UserSerializerV2'. Header-based versioning (Accept: application/vnd.myapi+json; version=1.0) is cleaner for clients. Deprecate old versions with response header: 'response["Sunset"] = "Sat, 01 Jan 2026 00:00:00 GMT"'.

Django's migration 'dependencies' field controls migration execution order. When two migrations in different apps both modify the same model (through ForeignKey relationships), failing to add explicit dependencies causes 'OperationalError: no such table' or 'column does not exist' on fresh database setup. The migration auto-detector adds dependencies for ForeignKey fields but not for data migrations or operations that implicitly depend on another app's schema. When writing data migrations, explicitly add dependencies: 'dependencies = [("app1", "0005_add_field"), ("app2", "0003_other")]'. Test 'migrate' from scratch on a clean database in CI to catch missing dependencies.

Django's 'data migrations' use 'RunPython' to execute Python code during schema migration. Pattern: 'def populate_slugs(apps, schema_editor): Post = apps.get_model("blog", "Post"); for post in Post.objects.all(): post.slug = slugify(post.title); post.save(update_fields=["slug"])'. Apply: 'migrations.RunPython(populate_slugs, reverse_code=migrations.RunPython.noop)'. Critical: always use 'apps.get_model()' not direct model import — the historical model state (not current code) is what's applied during migration. Provide a 'reverse_code' even if it's 'noop' to allow reversing. For large datasets, process in batches: 'Post.objects.iterator()' with periodic 'transaction.savepoint()'.

Django's 'aggregate()' with multiple aggregations on the same queryset can produce unexpected results when combined with 'annotate()'. Adding 'annotate(Count("related"))' before 'aggregate(Sum("amount"))' can inflate aggregate results due to JOINs multiplying rows. The safe order: use 'aggregate()' alone for grand totals, or use subqueries. For combined annotation + aggregation, use 'Subquery' expressions: 'annotate(total=Subquery(Order.objects.filter(user=OuterRef("pk")).values("user").annotate(s=Sum("amount")).values("s"))'. Django's ORM generates different SQL depending on annotation order; always verify with 'queryset.query' or Django Debug Toolbar.

Django's 'WhiteNoise' middleware enables efficient static file serving from Django itself in production without a separate CDN or Nginx configuration. Install 'whitenoise[brotli]' for Brotli compression. Add to MIDDLEWARE immediately after SecurityMiddleware: 'whitenoise.middleware.WhiteNoiseMiddleware'. Configure: 'STATICFILES_STORAGE = "whitenoise.storage.CompressedManifestStaticFilesStorage"'. Run 'collectstatic' to gather and compress files. WhiteNoise serves files with aggressive caching headers (versioned filenames from ManifestStaticFilesStorage) and handles Range requests, ETags, and conditional GETs. For very high traffic, still use a CDN in front, but WhiteNoise enables zero-config static file serving for smaller deployments.

Django's 'DATABASES["default"]["CONN_MAX_AGE"]' controls database connection persistence. The default is 0 (close connection after each request), which is safe but creates a new connection per request — expensive for high-traffic apps. Setting 'CONN_MAX_AGE = None' (persistent connections) or a high value can cause connection pool exhaustion on PostgreSQL (which has a 'max_connections' limit, often 100). With gunicorn 4 workers and CONN_MAX_AGE=500, you can hold up to 4 persistent connections per worker. For Django apps behind load balancers, use PgBouncer as a connection pooler rather than relying on CONN_MAX_AGE alone.

Django's 'django.test.override_settings' decorator and context manager allows per-test configuration overrides. '@override_settings(CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER=True)' makes Celery tasks synchronous in tests. '@override_settings(EMAIL_BACKEND="django.core.mail.backends.locmem.EmailBackend")' captures emails in 'mail.outbox'. '@override_settings(CACHES={"default": {"BACKEND": "django.core.cache.backends.dummy.DummyCache"}})' disables caching for cache-sensitive tests. For class-level: decorate the class. Overrides are automatically reversed after the test/scope. Use 'modify_settings' for list-type settings (like MIDDLEWARE) to add/remove items without replacing the whole list.

Django's 'reverse()' URL function fails with 'NoReverseMatch' when the URL pattern doesn't match the arguments provided. A common failure when using 'namespaced' URLs: 'reverse("profile")' fails when the URL is in a namespace — must use 'reverse("accounts:profile")'. Another failure: calling 'reverse()' at module level (outside a request context) works in most cases but fails for URLs that require arguments. In class-based views, 'self.get_success_url()' often uses 'reverse_lazy()' instead of 'reverse()' — lazy evaluation defers resolution until needed, preventing import-time failures.

Django REST Framework's 'nested writable serializers' enable creating related objects in a single API call. Override 'create()': 'def create(self, validated_data): items_data = validated_data.pop("items"); order = Order.objects.create(**validated_data); for item_data in items_data: OrderItem.objects.create(order=order, **item_data); return order'. Wrap in 'transaction.atomic()' for consistency. For nested serializer validation, the nested serializer is validated automatically by DRF before 'create()' is called. 'many=True' on nested serializers handles lists of related objects. Use 'allow_empty=False' to require at least one nested item. This pattern replaces multiple API calls with one atomic operation.

Django's 'STATIC_URL' and 'MEDIA_URL' must be different paths and both need to be served differently. Static files (CSS/JS/images): collected by 'collectstatic', served from 'STATIC_ROOT'. Media files (user uploads): stored in 'MEDIA_ROOT', served from 'MEDIA_URL'. In development, Django can serve media files when DEBUG=True with a URL pattern. In production, BOTH must be served by Nginx or a CDN — Django itself should not serve them. A common failure: setting up static file serving correctly but forgetting MEDIA_URL configuration, causing user uploads to be saved but never accessible via URLs. Always verify both serve correctly after deployment.

Django's 'postgresql-specific fields' from 'django.contrib.postgres' offer powerful capabilities beyond standard Django. 'ArrayField': store Python lists as PostgreSQL arrays with lookup operators ('contains', 'contained_by', 'overlap'). 'HStoreField': key-value store. 'RangeFields': date ranges, numeric ranges with overlap queries. 'CITextField/CICharField': case-insensitive text using PostgreSQL's citext extension. These fields use PostgreSQL-specific features and are not portable to other backends. Use 'ArrayField' for tags (avoid M2M for simple string tags). 'GenericIPAddressField' is portable; for IP range queries, use 'django.contrib.postgres.fields.networks'.

Django's 'signals are synchronous' — a slow signal receiver blocks the entire request. A 'post_save' signal that sends an HTTP request to a third-party API or processes images blocks the view until it completes. This is a common performance trap: adding slow operations to signals because it seems convenient. Rule: signal receivers should complete in under 10ms. For any slower operation, dispatch a Celery task instead. Also, exceptions in signal receivers propagate to the sender unless explicitly caught — a failing email signal can abort a database write if uncaught. Wrap signal receiver bodies in try/except.

Django's 'StreamingHttpResponse' handles large responses without loading everything into memory. Use for CSV exports, large file generation: 'def export_view(request): def generate_csv(): yield "id,name\n"; for row in MyModel.objects.values_list("id", "name").iterator(): yield f"{row[0]},{row[1]}\n"; response = StreamingHttpResponse(generate_csv(), content_type="text/csv"); response["Content-Disposition"] = "attachment; filename=export.csv"; return response'. Note: middleware that accesses 'response.content' (like GZIP middleware in some configurations) will force the generator to evaluate fully, defeating streaming. GZipMiddleware is safe because it wraps the iterator.

Django's 'ModelAdmin.readonly_fields' only makes fields non-editable in the admin interface; it does NOT prevent the field from being updated via the Django ORM or Django shell. Admin protections are for the admin UI only. A common security misconception: marking 'created_by' as readonly in admin thinking it prevents tampering — but anyone with Django shell access or using the ORM directly can modify it. For truly immutable fields, enforce immutability in the model's 'save()' method: check if the field has changed since creation and raise 'ValidationError' if someone tries to modify it.

Django's 'custom middleware' follows a callable protocol. Modern style using '__init__' and '__call__': 'class TimingMiddleware: def __init__(self, get_response): self.get_response = get_response; def __call__(self, request): start = time.time(); response = self.get_response(request); duration = time.time() - start; response["X-Duration"] = f"{duration:.3f}"; return response'. 'get_response' is the next middleware or view in the chain. For async support in Django 3.1+, implement 'async def __call__()' and set 'async_capable = True'. Synchronous middleware on async views causes thread pool overhead — implement both sync and async versions for performance-critical middleware.

Django REST Framework's 'request.data' is parsed lazily and can only be read once. Unlike Django's 'request.body', 'request.data' in DRF is a mutable QueryDict or dict depending on Content-Type. Accessing 'request.data' in a permission class (which runs before the view) is fine — DRF parses it once and caches it. However, if middleware reads 'request.body' before DRF processes the request, 'request.body' is consumed and subsequent reads return empty bytes.
