This is the same issue as Django's request.body stream consumption. In middleware, use 'request.body' and cache it: 'request._body = request.body' to allow re-reading.

Django's 'LOGGING' configuration enables structured, leveled logging throughout the application. Configure loggers per app: 'LOGGING = {"version": 1, "handlers": {"console": {"class": "logging.StreamHandler"}}, "loggers": {"django": {"handlers": ["console"], "level": "INFO"}, "myapp": {"handlers": ["console"], "level": "DEBUG", "propagate": False}}}'. Use 'django.db.backends' logger with DEBUG level to log all SQL (useful for development). In production, use 'logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler' or send to a log aggregator. Use 'extra' parameter for structured context: 'logger.info("User action", extra={"user_id": request.user.id, "action": "login"})'. Never use 'print()' in production Django code.

Django's 'migration autodetector' does not detect changes to 'validators' list on model fields — validators are not stored in migration state. If you add a validator to a field, 'makemigrations' won't generate a migration, and existing data won't be validated. Validators only run on 'full_clean()', forms, and DRF serializers. Database-level constraints (CheckConstraint, UniqueConstraint) DO generate migrations and are enforced by the database. Never rely solely on Django validators for data integrity on fields where the rule should always hold — pair with database constraints. This also means removing a validator doesn't require a migration.

Django's 'Subquery' and 'OuterRef' enable correlated subqueries for complex data retrieval patterns. Get the latest order date per user: 'User.objects.annotate(latest_order=Subquery(Order.objects.filter(user=OuterRef("pk")).order_by("-created_at").values("created_at")[:1]))'. 'OuterRef("pk")' references the outer query's primary key. Use 'Exists()' for boolean existence checks: 'User.objects.annotate(has_orders=Exists(Order.objects.filter(user=OuterRef("pk"))))'. Subqueries are evaluated in the database as correlated subqueries. For better performance on large datasets, consider 'prefetch_related' with 'Prefetch' and Python-side aggregation instead of subquery annotation.

Django's admin 'search_fields' with foreign key traversal can cause very slow queries on large tables. 'search_fields = ["user__email"]' generates a LIKE '%term%' query with a JOIN to the users table, which cannot use indexes effectively. On tables with millions of records, admin searches become unusable. Fix: use 'django-admin-searchable-dropdown' for foreign key searches, add database-level full text search indexes, or override 'get_search_results()' to implement efficient search. Always test admin search performance with production-size data in staging before going live.

Django's 'django-allauth' provides comprehensive authentication including social login, email verification, and account management. Configure providers: 'INSTALLED_APPS = ["allauth", "allauth.account", "allauth.socialaccount", "allauth.socialaccount.providers.google"]'. Settings: 'ACCOUNT_EMAIL_REQUIRED = True; ACCOUNT_AUTHENTICATION_METHOD = "email"; ACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION = "mandatory"'. Custom adapters override behavior: 'ACCOUNT_ADAPTER = "myapp.adapters.CustomAccountAdapter"'. For API-based auth (no HTML forms), use 'dj-rest-auth' with allauth backend — provides REST endpoints for login, registration, password reset. Override 'save_user()' in the adapter to set custom user fields during social login.

Django REST Framework's 'PrimaryKeyRelatedField' by default allows any object in the queryset as a valid value, without ownership validation. If 'queryset=Order.objects.all()' and a user submits another user's order ID, DRF validates it as valid. Always scope the queryset to the current user: override 'get_fields()' or use 'CurrentUserDefault': 'class OrderSerializer(ModelSerializer): user = serializers.HiddenField(default=serializers.CurrentUserDefault())' and scope related querysets in the view's 'get_serializer_context()'. Never trust that client-provided IDs reference objects the user owns without explicit ownership validation.

Django's 'django-redis' as cache backend enables full Redis features beyond simple caching. Configure: 'CACHES = {"default": {"BACKEND": "django_redis.cache.RedisCache", "LOCATION": "redis://127.0.0.1:6379/1", "OPTIONS": {"CLIENT_CLASS": "django_redis.client.DefaultClient", "SOCKET_TIMEOUT": 5}}}'. Access Redis client directly: 'from django_redis import get_redis_connection; con = get_redis_connection("default")'. Use 'con.incr("key")' for atomic counters, 'con.setex("key", 60, "value")' for time-limited keys. Configure 'DJANGO_REDIS_CLOSE_CONNECTION = True' for environments where connections should not persist across requests. Use 'SESSION_ENGINE = "django.contrib.sessions.backends.cache"' with Redis for scalable sessions.

Django's 'ORM QuerySet is lazy' — it doesn't execute SQL until evaluated (iteration, slicing, 'list()', 'len()', 'bool()'). Assigning a QuerySet to a variable and passing it to functions doesn't trigger evaluation. The failure mode: 'qs = MyModel.objects.filter(...)' followed by later code that modifies the underlying data and then evaluates the same 'qs' — the QuerySet re-executes the SQL at evaluation time, getting fresh data (which might differ from when it was created). Developers sometimes expect the QuerySet to capture a snapshot at assignment time. Cache the results explicitly: 'items = list(MyModel.objects.filter(...))' for a point-in-time snapshot.

Django's 'django-guardian' enables per-object permissions beyond Django's built-in per-type permissions. Assign object permission: 'assign_perm("view_article", user, article_instance)'. Check: 'user.has_perm("view_article", article_instance)'. Use 'ObjectPermissionMixin' in CBVs or 'DjangoObjectPermissions' in DRF. For large-scale permission systems, guardian stores permissions in two tables (one for users, one for groups) — queries become expensive with millions of permissions. Consider PostgreSQL's row-level security or attribute-based access control for very large datasets. Always combine object permissions with Django's built-in model-level permissions.

Django's 'TestClient' does not enforce 'ALLOWED_HOSTS' by default (Django sets 'SERVER_NAME' to 'testserver' and disables the check). Tests that check redirect URLs or absolute URLs using 'testserver' as hostname will pass locally but fail in production where the actual domain is different. Always test URL generation with realistic hostnames in integration tests. For testing ALLOWED_HOSTS specifically, use 'self.client.get("/path/", HTTP_HOST="invalid.example.com")' and verify the 400 response. Also, TestClient uses Django's test runner's database, not the actual database — migrations must be run before tests.

Django's 'django-mptt' or 'django-treebeard' handles hierarchical data (category trees, comment threads) efficiently. 'django-mptt' (Modified Preorder Tree Traversal): stores 'lft', 'rght', 'level', 'tree_id' for efficient subtree queries. 'treebeard' offers MP (Materialized Path), AL (Adjacency List), and NS (Nested Sets) strategies. For read-heavy trees: use NS or MP for O(1) subtree retrieval. For write-heavy trees: use AL (adjacency list) which has O(1) writes but O(n) reads. Simple category trees: 'mptt' is the most popular choice with good Django admin integration. Avoid storing trees as adjacency lists in plain ForeignKey(self) without a library — recursive queries get complex.

Django's 'session_key' is regenerated on 'request.session.cycle_key()' or 'login()' to prevent session fixation attacks. Failing to call 'cycle_key()' after authentication (e.g., after a successful 'authenticate()' call without using Django's 'login()' function) leaves the same session ID from pre-authentication state, enabling session fixation. Django's 'auth.login()' automatically calls 'cycle_key()'. If you manually authenticate and set session variables without calling 'login()', you must explicitly call 'request.session.cycle_key()'. This is a common oversight when implementing custom authentication flows.

Django's 'Sentry integration' via 'sentry-sdk' captures exceptions, performance traces, and user context automatically. Install: 'pip install sentry-sdk[django]'. Configure in settings: 'import sentry_sdk; sentry_sdk.init(dsn="https://...", integrations=[DjangoIntegration()], traces_sample_rate=0.1, send_default_pii=True)'. 'traces_sample_rate=0.1' captures 10% of transactions for performance monitoring. Sentry automatically captures Django signals, SQL queries (as breadcrumbs), and user information. 'send_default_pii=True' includes user email/username in error reports — ensure GDPR compliance. Add release tracking: 'release=os.environ.get("GIT_SHA")' to correlate errors with deployments.

Django's 'migration zero-downtime' practices are essential for production deployments. Adding a NOT NULL column without a default causes 'ALTER TABLE' to lock the table until all existing rows are backfilled — can lock for minutes on large tables. Safe approach: 1) Add nullable column (migration 1), 2) Backfill with a data migration, 3) Add constraint (migration 2), 4) Make not-null (migration 3). Use 'django-pg-zero-downtime-migrations' for PostgreSQL to ensure all migrations use safe ALTER TABLE statements. For renaming columns, use add_field + data_migration + remove_field pattern; never just rename — it's a drop and recreate in terms of data risk.

Django's 'django.contrib.postgres.indexes' provides PostgreSQL-specific index types for performance optimization. 'GinIndex': ideal for full-text search fields and ArrayField lookups. 'GistIndex': for geometric and range field queries. 'HashIndex': faster equality lookups for large string fields. 'BrinIndex': for large tables with naturally ordered data (timestamps, auto-increment IDs). 'SpGistIndex': for partitioned data. Usage: 'class Meta: indexes = [GinIndex(fields=["search_vector"], name="search_gin_idx"), GistIndex(fields=["date_range"], name="date_range_gist")]'. Standard B-tree index ('models.Index') covers most cases; use specialized indexes only after profiling confirms the need.

Django REST Framework's 'authentication_classes = []' on a view completely disables authentication — not just authentication enforcement. DRF first authenticates (populates request.user), then checks permissions. Setting empty authentication_classes means request.user is always AnonymousUser, even if the client sends valid credentials. Setting 'permission_classes = [AllowAny]' allows unauthenticated access while still populating request.user for authenticated clients. Use 'AllowAny' permission class to allow public access while preserving authentication context. Use empty authentication_classes only for truly public endpoints that must never identify users.

Django's 'select_for_update()' acquires row-level locks for the duration of the enclosing transaction. 'with transaction.atomic(): items = Item.objects.select_for_update().filter(order=order_id)' locks the selected rows. Other transactions attempting to modify these rows block until the lock is released. 'nowait=True' raises 'OperationalError' immediately if a lock can't be acquired (non-blocking). 'skip_locked=True' skips already-locked rows — perfect for job queues: 'Job.objects.select_for_update(skip_locked=True).filter(status="pending")[:1]' safely dequeues jobs without blocking. Never hold select_for_update locks across slow operations (external API calls, file I/O).

Django's 'SECRET_KEY_FALLBACKS' (Django 4.1+) enables key rotation without immediately invalidating all sessions, CSRF tokens, and signed cookies. A common failure during key rotation: changing SECRET_KEY without fallbacks instantly invalidates all active sessions, logging out all users. Proper rotation: 1) Move current key to SECRET_KEY_FALLBACKS, 2) Generate new SECRET_KEY, 3) Deploy, 4) After sufficient time (session lifetime), remove old key from fallbacks. Without this procedure, a key rotation causes a mass logout event visible to all users. Django signs cookies and tokens with the primary SECRET_KEY but validates against fallbacks too.

Django's 'raw()' queryset method enables raw SQL queries while returning model instances. 'users = User.objects.raw("SELECT * FROM auth_user WHERE age > %s", [min_age])'. Raw queries return 'RawQuerySet' which is iterable but doesn't support most QuerySet methods (filter, annotate, etc.). Use 'cursor.execute()' for non-SELECT queries or when you don't need model instances: 'from django.db import connection; with connection.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("UPDATE ...")'. Always use parameterized queries (% substitution) never string formatting to prevent SQL injection. Reserve raw SQL for performance-critical queries that Django's ORM cannot generate efficiently.

Django REST Framework's 'Serializer.is_valid()' returns False on validation errors but does NOT raise an exception. Code that doesn't check 'if serializer.is_valid():' before calling 'serializer.save()' raises 'AssertionError: You must call .is_valid() before calling .save()'. The pattern 'serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)' is the recommended approach for views — it raises 'ValidationError' which DRF's exception handler converts to a 400 response with error details automatically. Without 'raise_exception=True', you must manually handle errors: 'if not serializer.is_valid(): return Response(serializer.errors, status=400)'. Use 'raise_exception=True' consistently in views.

Django's 'model forms' (ModelForm) automatically generate form fields from model definitions. 'class ArticleForm(ModelForm): class Meta: model = Article; fields = ["title", "content", "category"]; widgets = {"content": Textarea(attrs={"rows": 10})}'. Use 'fields = "__all__"' cautiously — it exposes all model fields including internal ones. Use 'exclude' for removing specific fields: 'exclude = ["author", "created_at"]'. 'form.save(commit=False)' creates but doesn't save the instance, allowing modification before saving: 'article = form.save(commit=False); article.author = request.user; article.save()'. Always use 'fields' or 'exclude' explicitly to prevent mass assignment vulnerabilities.

Django's 'QuerySet caching' only applies to already-evaluated querysets. A queryset is cached after first evaluation, but subsequent filters or modifications create new querysets that are not cached. 'qs = User.objects.all(); list(qs); qs.filter(active=True)' — the second filter creates a NEW queryset and hits the database again. Developers sometimes assume all operations on 'qs' use the cached data. Also, model instances have an internal prefetch cache — accessing 'user.groups.all()' twice on the same instance uses the cache only if 'prefetch_related' was used; otherwise, it queries twice. Use 'refresh_from_db()' explicitly to force refresh when needed.

Django's 'django-model-utils' provides useful model utilities beyond core Django. 'TimeStampedModel': abstract model with 'created' and 'modified' fields. 'StatusField' and 'Choices': type-safe status field with 'monitor_field': 'status = StatusField(choices_name="STATUS"); STATUS = Choices("draft", "published")'. 'StatusModel': combines status field with 'QueryManagerMixin'. 'SplitField': efficient splitting of large text for summary vs full content. 'PassThroughManager': enables custom QuerySet methods on the manager. 'InheritanceManager' and 'InheritanceQuerySet': automatically retrieves subclass instances in multi-table inheritance. These utilities reduce boilerplate for common Django model patterns.

Django's 'admin.site.register()' without a custom ModelAdmin class uses the default admin which shows all model fields in list and detail views. This exposes potentially sensitive fields (password hashes, internal tokens, flags) in the admin panel. Always define a ModelAdmin class with explicit 'list_display', 'list_filter', 'search_fields', 'readonly_fields', and 'exclude'. Also: Django admin does not enforce row-level permissions by default — all staff users can see and edit all objects of registered models. Use 'get_queryset()' override to filter objects by ownership if needed. Treat the admin as a power tool, not a secure interface.

Django's 'Conditional GET' support (If-Modified-Since, ETag) reduces bandwidth for unchanged content. Use 'ConditionalGetMiddleware' (in default MIDDLEWARE) which handles ETags automatically for most views. For custom ETag generation: '@condition(etag_func=my_etag_func) def my_view(request): ...'. The etag function receives the same arguments as the view and returns an ETag string. Use 'last_modified_func' for Last-Modified based caching. For DRF views, implement 'get_etag()' or use 'ConditionalGetMixin'. This dramatically reduces load for APIs serving mostly-static data that clients poll frequently.

Django's 'forms.FileField' and 'request.FILES' provide the uploaded file as an 'InMemoryUploadedFile' or 'TemporaryUploadedFile' depending on size (configurable with 'FILE_UPLOAD_MAX_MEMORY_SIZE'). A common failure: accessing 'uploaded_file.read()' and then passing the same file object elsewhere — the file pointer is at the end after reading. Call 'uploaded_file.seek(0)' before re-reading. Temporary files are deleted when the request ends — process them within the request or copy to permanent storage. Never store the path to a temporary upload file for later processing; copy the content or save it to a model's FileField immediately.

Django's 'django-rest-framework-simplejwt' provides JWT authentication for DRF APIs. Configure: 'REST_FRAMEWORK = {"DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES": ["rest_framework_simplejwt.authentication.JWTAuthentication"]}'. Add token endpoints to URLs: 'path("api/token/", TokenObtainPairView.as_view()), path("api/token/refresh/", TokenRefreshView.as_view())'. Customize token lifetime: 'SIMPLE_JWT = {"ACCESS_TOKEN_LIFETIME": timedelta(minutes=5), "REFRESH_TOKEN_LIFETIME": timedelta(days=7)}'. Add custom claims: subclass 'TokenObtainPairSerializer' and override 'get_token()'. For token blacklisting on logout: enable 'rest_framework_simplejwt.token_blacklist' app. Use short-lived access tokens with refresh to minimize exposure window.

Django's 'test isolation' is broken when tests use 'setUpClass()' instead of 'setUpTestData()' — 'setUpClass()' doesn't have transaction rollback support built in. Data created in 'setUpClass()' persists across all tests in the class AND bleeds into other test classes if the test runner doesn't reset the database. Use 'setUpTestData()' for class-scoped test data with automatic rollback. Also, test isolation breaks when tests use signals that create side effects not cleaned up after each test. Use 'mock.patch' or 'disconnect' signals in setUp/tearDown to prevent signal side effects from polluting other tests.

Django's 'db_index=True' creates a B-tree index on a field; composite indexes via 'Meta.indexes' cover multi-column scenarios. Partial indexes (PostgreSQL) via 'Index(condition=Q(...))' reduce index size and improve query performance for filtered queries. Covering indexes include additional columns to avoid table lookups: 'Index(fields=["status"], include=["created_at", "user_id"])'. Expression-based indexes: 'Index(Lower("email"), name="email_lower_idx")' supports case-insensitive email lookups without calling 'lower()' in Python. Use 'CONCURRENTLY' for adding indexes to live databases: override migration to use 'database_forwards()' with 'CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY'.

Django's 'FIXTURE' loading with 'loaddata' is order-dependent for ForeignKey relationships. Fixtures must be loaded in order of their dependencies (parent before child). Using 'natural keys' in fixtures avoids this by using human-readable identifiers instead of PKs, but natural keys require the model to implement 'natural_key()' and 'NaturalKeyManager'. A common CI failure: fixtures load correctly in development because of lucky ordering, but fail in CI where tables are empty and loading order is different. Use factory_boy instead of fixtures for most test data — factories handle dependencies automatically and are more maintainable than JSON fixtures.

Django's 'Paginator' class handles pagination of querysets or lists. Usage: 'paginator = Paginator(object_list, per_page=25); page_obj = paginator.get_page(request.GET.get("page"))'. 'get_page()' handles out-of-range page numbers gracefully (returns first/last page). 'page()' raises 'EmptyPage' or 'InvalidPage' for invalid page numbers. Template integration: 'page_obj.has_next()', 'page_obj.next_page_number()'. For DRF, use DRF's built-in 'PageNumberPagination'. For cursor-based pagination (better for large datasets), use 'CursorPagination' — stable even when records are added/deleted between page loads. Avoid 'OFFSET N' based pagination for tables with millions of rows — use keyset pagination.

Django REST Framework's 'permission_classes' applied to a ViewSet apply to ALL actions including 'list', 'create', 'retrieve', 'update', 'destroy'. If you want different permissions per action (e.g., allow unauthenticated read but require auth for write), override 'get_permissions()': 'def get_permissions(self): if self.action in ["list", "retrieve"]: return [AllowAny()]; return [IsAuthenticated()]'. A common failure: applying 'IsAuthenticated' globally to a ModelViewSet and wondering why public read endpoints return 401. The 'permission_classes = [IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly]' shortcut works for simple cases but 'get_permissions()' provides full control per action.

Django's 'admin actions' enable bulk operations on selected admin list items. Define: 'def make_published(modeladmin, request, queryset): queryset.update(status="published"); make_published.short_description = "Mark selected articles as published"'. Register: 'class ArticleAdmin(ModelAdmin): actions = [make_published]'. For actions requiring confirmation or additional input, use 'intermediate page' pattern: return a response from the action function to render a custom template. Actions have access to 'modeladmin' for permissions checks, 'request' for user context, and 'queryset' of selected items. Always add permissions checks in actions: 'if not request.user.has_perm("app.change_article"): return'.

Django's 'get_user_model()' can fail with 'django.contrib.auth.get_user_model has not been called' if called before Django's app registry is fully initialized. This happens at module level (top of file), in metaclass code, or in migration files that import user model directly. Always import 'get_user_model' and call it inside functions, not at module level. In migrations, use 'settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL' string for ForeignKey: 'ForeignKey(settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL, on_delete=CASCADE)' — never import the User model directly in migrations as the model state may differ from current code.

Django's 'Cache framework with versioning' enables cache invalidation without flushing all keys. Set version: 'cache.set("key", value, version=2)'. Get with version: 'cache.get("key", version=2)'. Increment version to invalidate: 'cache.incr_version("key")' or set a new version globally in settings. Cache prefixes prevent key collisions in shared Redis instances: 'CACHES = {"default": {"LOCATION": "redis://...", "KEY_PREFIX": "myapp"}}'. For cache stampede prevention (many requests hitting DB simultaneously on cache miss), use probabilistic early expiration: set a slightly shorter TTL than the actual expiration and 'regenerate' with some probability before expiry.

Django's 'prefetch_related' cache is invalidated when you modify the model instance. Calling 'user.orders.add(order)' or 'user.orders.clear()' after prefetching clears the prefetch cache. Subsequent accesses to 'user.orders.all()' will execute a new database query. This is correct behavior but surprises developers who modify relationships mid-loop expecting the prefetch cache to stay current. Also: accessing 'user.orders.filter(status="active")' after prefetching 'orders' does NOT use the prefetch cache — it executes a new query. The prefetch cache only serves calls to 'user.orders.all()' exactly.

Django REST Framework's 'Viewset.get_object()' method automatically calls 'check_object_permissions()' which triggers 'has_object_permission()' on all permission classes. Bypassing 'get_object()' and fetching the object directly (e.g., 'obj = MyModel.objects.get(pk=pk)') skips object-level permission checks. Always use 'self.get_object()' in ViewSet methods to ensure all permission checks run. For custom retrieval logic, override 'get_object()' and call 'self.check_object_permissions(self.request, obj)' explicitly after fetching the object: 'obj = MyModel.objects.get(pk=pk); self.check_object_permissions(self.request, obj); return obj'.

Django's 'upload_to' parameter in FileField/ImageField can be a callable for dynamic paths: 'def user_directory_path(instance, filename): return f"uploads/{instance.user.pk}/{filename}"'. However, when the model instance is being created for the first time, 'instance.pk' might be None if the instance hasn't been saved yet. Using 'instance.pk' in 'upload_to' during initial creation generates 'None' in the path. Fix: use 'instance.user.pk' (FK to an existing object) or use a UUID field as part of the path. For updating file fields, the old file is NOT deleted — you must handle old file cleanup explicitly to avoid storage accumulation.

Django's 'email sending' with 'django.core.mail' integrates with any SMTP server or email service API. 'send_mail(subject, message, from_email, to_list)' for simple emails. 'EmailMultiAlternatives' for HTML + text fallback: 'msg = EmailMultiAlternatives(subject, text, from, to); msg.attach_alternative(html_content, "text/html"); msg.send()'. Configure backend: 'EMAIL_BACKEND = "django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend"'. In development: 'EMAIL_BACKEND = "django.core.mail.backends.console.EmailBackend"' (prints to console). In tests: 'EMAIL_BACKEND = "django.core.mail.backends.locmem.EmailBackend"' with 'mail.outbox'. For production: use 'anymail' library for transactional email services (SendGrid, Mailgun, Postmark) with delivery tracking.

Django's 'signals.disconnect()' must be called with the exact same receiver function reference used in 'connect()'. If the signal was connected using a lambda, you cannot disconnect it by creating a new identical lambda — lambdas have different identities each time they're created. The correct pattern for disconnectable signal connections: store the connected function: 'def handler(sender, **kwargs): ...; post_save.connect(handler, sender=MyModel)'. To disconnect: 'post_save.disconnect(handler, sender=MyModel)'. In tests, use 'mock.patch.object' or 'unittest.mock' to temporarily replace signal receivers, which automatically restores them after the test.

Django's 'permission system' separates 'model permissions' (view, add, change, delete for each model) from 'custom permissions'. Django auto-creates 4 permissions per model during migrations. Add custom permissions: 'class Meta: permissions = [("can_publish", "Can publish articles"), ("can_feature", "Can feature articles")]'. Check: 'request.user.has_perm("blog.can_publish")'. Assign in admin or code: 'user.user_permissions.add(Permission.objects.get(codename="can_publish"))'. Group-based permissions: assign permissions to groups, add users to groups. For 'staff' users (is_staff=True), grant access to specific admin sections via ModelAdmin.has_*_permission() methods.

Django's 'double-save' anti-pattern in signals: a 'post_save' signal handler that calls 'instance.save()' triggers another 'post_save' signal, causing infinite recursion or at minimum double the database writes. Use 'update_fields' to prevent re-triggering: 'if not kwargs.get("update_fields"): instance.update_fields(["computed_field"])'. Use 'update_fields=["status"]' in the signal to mark it as a targeted update, then check in the signal: 'if update_fields and "status" in update_fields: return'. Better: avoid modifying the originating model in its own post_save signal. Use 'pre_save' to modify the instance before the original save, not after.

Django's 'django-cors-headers' handles Cross-Origin Resource Sharing for APIs consumed by frontend SPAs. Install and configure: 'INSTALLED_APPS = ["corsheaders"]; MIDDLEWARE = ["corsheaders.middleware.CorsMiddleware", ...]'. 'CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS = ["https://app.example.com"]'. For credentials: 'CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS = True' and never use 'CORS_ALLOW_ALL_ORIGINS = True' simultaneously — browsers block this combination. Whitelist specific headers: 'CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS = list(default_headers) + ["X-Custom-Header"]'. For preflight caching: 'CORS_PREFLIGHT_MAX_AGE = 86400' (24h). Place 'CorsMiddleware' before any middleware that generates responses (like authentication middleware) to ensure OPTIONS requests are handled correctly.

Django's 'bulk_update()' (Django 2.2+) requires 'update_fields' to be specified explicitly — unlike 'save()' which updates all fields by default. Calling 'MyModel.objects.bulk_update(objects, ["status", "updated_at"])' is correct. Omitting fields (not providing the list) raises TypeError. Also, 'bulk_update()' does not call 'save()' (same as 'bulk_create'), so validators, signals, and custom save logic don't run. For large updates where signals are needed, process in batches: chunk the queryset and call 'save()' per object. 'bulk_update()' with very large batch sizes (thousands of objects) can generate SQL that exceeds database parameter limits.

Django's 'django-ninja' is an alternative REST framework with FastAPI-like syntax. Define API: 'api = NinjaAPI(); @api.get("/users/{user_id}", response=UserSchema) def get_user(request, user_id: int): return User.objects.get(pk=user_id)'. Pydantic v2 for schema validation, automatic OpenAPI generation, async support, and type annotations. Compare to DRF: django-ninja has faster development cycle and automatic OpenAPI, while DRF has larger ecosystem and more third-party packages. Use django-ninja for new projects requiring clean API design; use DRF for projects needing extensive third-party integrations (OAuth, JWT, DRF extensions ecosystem).

Django's 'DATABASES setting with SSL requires' 'OPTIONS': 'DATABASES = {"default": {"ENGINE": "django.db.backends.postgresql", "OPTIONS": {"sslmode": "require"}}}'. Without 'sslmode': 'require', connections to PostgreSQL may fall back to unencrypted connections, especially when connecting to managed database services (AWS RDS, Heroku Postgres) that support both. Many developers assume SSL is automatic but it requires explicit configuration. Use 'sslmode': 'verify-full' with CA certificate for maximum security in production. Check active connections' SSL status with 'SELECT ssl FROM pg_stat_ssl WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid()'.

Django's 'Wagtail CMS' integrates cleanly with Django for content-managed sites. Wagtail extends Django's model system with 'Page' base class and a tree-based content hierarchy via 'django-treebeard'. Define custom page types: 'class BlogPage(Page): body = StreamField([...]); content_panels = Page.content_panels + [FieldPanel("body")]'. StreamField enables rich, structured content without a fixed schema. Wagtail's admin ('wagtailcore.admin.sites') is separate from Django admin. Deploy Django + Wagtail together on the same gunicorn/uwsgi server. Use Wagtail for marketing pages and blog while keeping Django REST Framework for the application API — they coexist naturally.

Django REST Framework's 'ModelSerializer.validate_{field_name}()' validators run per-field after type coercion. A common mistake: 'validate_email()' attempting to check database uniqueness by calling 'User.objects.filter(email=value).exists()' — this doesn't account for the case where the user is updating their own email (the current email would appear as a duplicate). For update scenarios, access the instance via 'self.instance': 'if User.objects.filter(email=value).exclude(pk=self.instance.pk if self.instance else None).exists(): raise ValidationError(...)'. Always handle both create (no instance) and update (has instance) in uniqueness validators.

Django's 'AbstractUser vs AbstractBaseUser': 'AbstractUser' keeps Django's default fields (username, email, first_name, last_name, etc.) and adds your custom fields on top. 'AbstractBaseUser' gives you a bare minimum (only password and last_login) and requires implementing all user fields and the custom UserManager. Use 'AbstractUser' when you want email login but keep the existing user structure: just override 'USERNAME_FIELD = "email"' and 'REQUIRED_FIELDS = []'. Use 'AbstractBaseUser' only when you need radically different user structure (e.g., UUID instead of integer PK, no username concept, completely different required fields). Don't start with AbstractBaseUser unless you're certain AbstractUser won't work.

Django's 'cache_page' decorator doesn't work correctly with views that return different content for different users (authentication-dependent views). '@cache_page(300)' caches the first response and serves it to ALL subsequent visitors, regardless of authentication state. A logged-in user's private content may be served to anonymous users. Fix: use 'vary_on_cookie()' or 'vary_on_headers("Authorization")' decorators to key the cache per authentication state, or use low-level caching with user-specific keys. 'cache_page' is safe only for truly public, anonymous-accessible content that is identical for all users.

Django's 'RunSQL' in migrations executes raw SQL and is the appropriate tool for operations not supported by Django's schema editor: creating triggers, stored procedures, complex constraints, renaming columns, creating custom types. 'migrations.RunSQL("CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_name ON mytable(col)", reverse_sql="DROP INDEX idx_name", hints={"blocking": False})'. The 'hints' dict can signal that this migration is safe to run concurrently without locking the table. Always provide 'reverse_sql' for reversible operations. For multiple SQL statements: use a list of strings. Wrap non-transactional SQL (CONCURRENTLY) in 'non_atomic_migrations': 'class Migration(migrations.Migration): atomic = False'.

Django REST Framework's 'ListSerializer' handles many=True serializer behavior. When using 'many=True', DRF creates a 'ListSerializer' wrapping your serializer. Overriding 'create()' or 'update()' in the child serializer doesn't affect many=True behavior — you must override 'many_init()' class method or subclass 'ListSerializer' and specify 'list_serializer_class'. A common failure: expecting 'MySerializer(data=list_data, many=True).save()' to call 'MySerializer.create()' for each item — it actually calls 'ListSerializer.create()' which calls child.create() per item. For bulk update with many=True, this is especially tricky to implement correctly.

Django's 'multi-tenancy with row-level isolation' uses a 'current tenant' context to filter all queries automatically. Implement via custom middleware and thread-local storage: 'middleware sets CURRENT_TENANT; custom manager adds .filter(tenant=get_current_tenant()) to all querysets'. Use 'django-tenant-schemas' or 'django-tenants' for PostgreSQL schema-based isolation (stronger isolation, more complex setup). Row-level: simpler but requires all models to have a tenant FK. Schema-level: complete data isolation, easy to add new tenants. For GDPR/data sovereignty requirements, schema-level is often required. Test multi-tenancy isolation rigorously — a cross-tenant data leak is a critical security vulnerability.

Django's 'ManyRelatedManager.add()' and 'set()' for M2M fields bypass the 'through' model when one exists. If you define a through model, you cannot use 'user.groups.add(group)' — you must create the through model instance directly: 'UserGroup.objects.create(user=user, group=group, role="admin")'. The 'add()' method raises 'AttributeError' when a through model with extra fields exists. This is a common confusion when adding a through model to an existing M2M relationship — existing code using 'add()' breaks. Audit all M2M relationship usage before adding a through model.

Django REST Framework's 'renderer classes' control output format. DRF returns JSON by default; add browsable API and other formats: 'REST_FRAMEWORK = {"DEFAULT_RENDERER_CLASSES": ["rest_framework.renderers.JSONRenderer", "rest_framework.renderers.BrowsableAPIRenderer"]}'. CSV renderer: install 'djangorestframework-csv'. The browsable API renderer is excellent for development but disable in production: 'DEFAULT_RENDERER_CLASSES = ["rest_framework.renderers.JSONRenderer"] if not DEBUG else [...]'. Custom renderers: subclass 'BaseRenderer', define 'media_type' and 'render()'. Content negotiation: clients specify desired format via 'Accept: application/json' header or '?format=csv' query parameter.

Django's 'IntegerChoices' and 'TextChoices' (Django 3.0+) provide enum-like behavior for choice fields. 'class Status(models.IntegerChoices): DRAFT = 0, "Draft"; PUBLISHED = 1, "Published"'. Use: 'status = models.IntegerField(choices=Status.choices, default=Status.DRAFT)'. The failure mode: legacy code using magic numbers (status=0, status=1) instead of 'Status.DRAFT' — when the integer values are reordered in the enum, all existing code breaks silently. Always use the symbolic name: 'Article.objects.filter(status=Status.PUBLISHED)'. Migration note: changing enum values requires a data migration to update existing database values.

Django's 'test fixtures with pytest-django' follow different conventions than Django's built-in TestCase. 'pytest.mark.django_db' decorator grants database access: '@pytest.mark.django_db def test_create_user(): user = User.objects.create_user("test", "test@example.com", "pass")'. Use 'db' fixture for transaction rollback (like TestCase) or 'django_db_allow_db_reuse' for TransactionTestCase behavior. 'client' fixture provides Django test client. 'settings' fixture enables settings overrides. 'django_assert_num_queries' fixture checks exact query count. Pytest-django fixtures compose with factory_boy for clean test data. Use '@pytest.mark.django_db(transaction=True)' for tests requiring actual transaction commit (Celery on_commit testing).

Django's 'admin customization' with 'inline models' creates N+1 queries when displaying related objects. 'TabularInline' for a model with 20 related objects executes a SELECT per inline object by default. Fix: define 'get_queryset()' on the inline to add 'select_related()' or 'prefetch_related()'. Also, 'raw_id_fields' for ForeignKey fields in inlines prevents loading ALL related objects in a dropdown — critical when the related model has thousands of records: 'raw_id_fields = ("product",)' shows a text input with a popup search instead. Monitor admin performance separately from public API — admin N+1 queries are a common source of admin timeouts.

Django's 'Channels + WebSocket consumer authentication' requires manual token validation since WebSocket handshake doesn't support cookie-based auth across different origins. 'JWTAuthMiddleware': intercept the ASGI connect scope, extract token from query string or first message, validate, and attach user to scope. 'class JWTAuthMiddleware: async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send): token = parse_querystring(scope["query_string"]).get("token"); scope["user"] = await get_user_from_token(token); return await self.inner(scope, receive, send)'. In the consumer: 'self.scope["user"]'. For browser clients, send JWT in query string on WebSocket connect; for native apps, send in first message.

Django's 'class Meta: ordering' on a model applies a default ordering to all querysets. While convenient, this ordering adds an ORDER BY clause to every query on the model, even queries where ordering doesn't matter (existence checks, aggregations, update operations). This can prevent index usage and slow down queries significantly. 'exists()' and 'count()' queries are affected: 'MyModel.objects.filter(...).order_by().count()' is faster than 'MyModel.objects.filter(...).count()' when Meta.ordering is set. Clear ordering for bulk operations: 'MyModel.objects.all().order_by()'. Consider removing Meta.ordering and adding explicit '.order_by()' only where needed.

Django's 'Haystack' or 'elasticsearch-dsl-django' enable full-featured search with Elasticsearch. For simpler full-text search without Elasticsearch: PostgreSQL's built-in full-text search via 'SearchVector' (covered separately). For Elasticsearch: 'elasticsearch-dsl-django' provides Pythonic DSL for defining indexes and querying. Define document: '@registry.register_document class ArticleDocument(Document): class Index: name = "articles"; class Django: model = Article; fields = ["title", "body", "author"]'. Search: 'ArticleDocument.search().query("multi_match", query=q, fields=["title^2", "body"])'. Sync data with Django signals or periodic management commands.

Django's 'ATOMIC_REQUESTS = True' combined with database constraints can produce confusing error messages. When a view raises an IntegrityError inside an atomic request, Django catches it, marks the transaction as needing rollback, and returns a 500 error. The original IntegrityError is logged but the HTTP response doesn't convey what went wrong. Developers see generic 500 errors in production when there are duplicate inserts, null constraint violations, etc. Solution: catch IntegrityError explicitly inside views and return appropriate 400/409 responses. Use 'transaction.savepoint()' to protect specific operations without aborting the entire request transaction.

Django's 'drf-spectacular with django-ninja comparison' for API documentation: drf-spectacular generates OpenAPI 3.0 from DRF code with explicit '@extend_schema' decorators for disambiguation. Django-ninja generates OpenAPI natively from type annotations. For existing DRF projects: add drf-spectacular. For new projects: consider django-ninja for cleaner OpenAPI. Both support: request/response schema generation, authentication documentation, pagination schemas. Key drf-spectacular pattern: '@extend_schema(summary="List users", description="Returns paginated user list", responses={200: UserSerializer(many=True), 403: None})'. Always commit the generated schema and check for drift in CI.

Django's 'migration conflicts' occur when two developers create migrations in the same app simultaneously based on the same parent migration. Both migrations will have the same 'dependencies' but different operations. Running 'migrate' with both fails. Resolution: 'python manage.py makemigrations --merge' creates a merge migration that depends on both conflicting migrations. However, if the conflicting migrations touch the same table, the merge might have ordering issues. Prevention: always pull and run 'migrate' before starting work; use feature flags for parallel feature development to avoid parallel schema changes; communicate schema changes in team channels.

Django's 'django-ratelimit' provides request rate limiting at the view level without requiring nginx configuration. '@ratelimit(key="ip", rate="10/m", block=True)' limits to 10 requests per minute by IP. For authenticated users: '@ratelimit(key="user", rate="100/h")'. Custom keys: '@ratelimit(key="header:X-API-Key", rate="1000/d")'. Use 'RATELIMIT_USE_CACHE = "default"' to store counters in Django's cache. 'block=False' doesn't block but sets 'request.limited = True' for soft-limiting (log but allow). For API keys with different tiers, implement custom rate key function that returns different rates based on key tier. Requires Redis or Memcached backend for cross-process rate limiting.

Django's 'LANGUAGE_CODE' and 'USE_I18N' settings control internationalization. A common failure: deploying a Django app with 'USE_TZ = False' (timezone-naive datetimes) and then enabling it ('USE_TZ = True') after data exists in the database — all existing naive datetimes are now treated as UTC by Django but may actually be local time. Never change 'USE_TZ' after data is stored. Start all projects with 'USE_TZ = True'. Another failure: 'USE_I18N = False' disables translation but you've hardcoded strings in views — later enabling i18n requires retrofitting all strings with _('...'), which is a significant refactor.

Django's 'contenttypes framework with django-activity-stream' enables generic activity/audit logging. Activity: '@receiver(post_save, sender=Article) def log_activity(sender, instance, created, **kwargs): Action.objects.create(actor=current_user(), verb="created" if created else "updated", action_object=instance)'. 'django-activity-stream' provides 'Action' model with polymorphic actor, verb, target, action_object via GenericForeignKey. Query: 'user.actor_actions.all()' for all user actions; 'target_stream(article)' for all actions on specific article. Use for user-facing activity feeds and admin audit trails. For compliance-required audit logs, use a separate immutable log table with 'INSERT ONLY' permissions.

Django REST Framework's 'action decorator' with 'detail=False' creates list-level actions (e.g., '/users/bulk_create/') and 'detail=True' creates object-level actions (e.g., '/users/123/activate/'). A common failure: mismatching 'detail=True/False' with the action's logic — using 'detail=True' for a bulk action that doesn't need a pk, causing all callers to include a pk in the URL unnecessarily. Also, actions with 'methods=["get"]' will appear in the URL router but won't be documented in schema unless you add '@extend_schema'. DRF's DefaultRouter generates both list and detail URLs automatically from registered ViewSets.

Django's 'django.test.Client' supports multipart form uploads and JSON requests. JSON: 'self.client.post("/api/users/", {"name": "test"}, content_type="application/json")'. File upload: 'self.client.post("/upload/", {"file": open("test.pdf", "rb")}, format="multipart")'. For DRF-specific testing: 'from rest_framework.test import APIClient; client = APIClient(); client.force_authenticate(user=user); response = client.post("/api/users/", {"name": "test"}, format="json")'. 'force_authenticate()' bypasses all authentication and is the cleanest way to test authenticated endpoints. Use 'client.credentials(HTTP_AUTHORIZATION="Bearer " + token)' to test the actual authentication flow.

Django's 'related_name' conflict occurs when two ForeignKeys from the same model point to the same target model. 'class Comment(models.Model): post = ForeignKey(Post, related_name="comments"); reply_to = ForeignKey(Post, related_name="replies")'. Without unique 'related_name', Django raises a system check error: 'Reverse accessor clash'. When using abstract models with ForeignKeys, use '%(class)s' in related_name to make them unique per subclass: 'ForeignKey(Post, related_name="%(class)s_comments")'. This generates 'articlecomment_set', 'photocomment_set' etc. on the target model. Always define explicit 'related_name' — never rely on Django's automatic '_set' naming.

Django's 'middleware vs context processors': middleware runs before and after every request; context processors run only for template-rendered responses and add data to the template context. Use middleware for: authentication, security headers, request timing, routing decisions. Use context processors for: user notifications, navigation items, site settings that templates need everywhere. Context processors receive 'request' and return a dict merged into template context. Register: 'TEMPLATES[0]["OPTIONS"]["context_processors"].append("myapp.context_processors.notifications")'. Performance note: context processors run on every template response — keep them fast and cache expensive lookups.

Django's 'ORM .update()' method bypasses model validation, signals, and 'save()' method entirely. 'Article.objects.filter(pk=1).update(title="new")' skips 'pre_save'/'post_save' signals, 'clean()'/'full_clean()', and custom 'save()' logic. This is intentional for performance but surprises developers who rely on signals or save() for derived field updates. A specific failure: using 'update()' on a model with a 'modified_at = auto_now=True' field — 'update()' does NOT trigger auto_now, so the modification timestamp stays stale. Use 'update()' only when you explicitly don't need signals/validation, and pass 'updated_at=timezone.now()' explicitly when needed.

Django's 'database connection management' in Celery workers requires explicit handling. Django closes database connections automatically at the end of each HTTP request, but Celery tasks run outside HTTP request lifecycle. Use 'django.db.close_old_connections()' at the start of long-running periodic tasks to ensure fresh connections. 'django_db_close_connection' or configure 'django.db.utils.OperationalError' retry logic for transient connection failures. In Celery, use 'signals.task_prerun.connect' to call 'db.connections.close_all()' before each task if using persistent workers. For high-frequency tasks, configure 'CONN_MAX_AGE' appropriately to avoid connection pool exhaustion.

Django's 'admin URL security': the default admin URL '/admin/' is well-known and targeted by bots. Move it: 'path("secret-path/", admin.site.urls)'. But 'security through obscurity' is not real security — always add: 'ADMIN_MEDIA_PREFIX' settings, require 2FA for admin users ('django-two-factor-auth'), restrict by IP via middleware or Nginx, and use 'SECURE_ADMIN_LOGIN' settings. 'staff' access does not equal 'superuser' — audit who has is_staff=True. Never give all staff users 'is_superuser=True'. Regularly audit admin users: staff accounts should be service accounts with minimal permissions, not personal accounts.

Django's 'django-import-export' provides admin integration for importing/exporting data via CSV, Excel, and JSON. Define resource: 'class ArticleResource(resources.ModelResource): class Meta: model = Article; import_id_fields = ["slug"]; skip_unchanged = True; report_skipped = True'. Register in admin: 'class ArticleAdmin(ImportExportModelAdmin): resource_classes = [ArticleResource]'. Use 'import_id_fields' to identify records for update (not PK) — prevents creating duplicates on re-import. 'skip_unchanged=True' makes re-imports idempotent. For large imports, use background task via Celery: override 'import_data()' to return task ID and poll for completion.

Django REST Framework's 'pagination prevents count queries' — but 'PageNumberPagination' executes a COUNT(*) query to calculate 'total' pages. For very large tables, COUNT(*) is slow (PostgreSQL must scan indexes or tables). Solutions: disable count with custom pagination: 'class NoPaginationCountMixin: def paginate_queryset(self, qs, request, view=None): ... (skip count); use "next"/"previous" only'. CursorPagination avoids count entirely. For tables with millions of rows, estimate count via PostgreSQL statistics: 'SELECT reltuples FROM pg_class WHERE relname = "myapp_mymodel"' (not exact but fast). Always profile pagination performance on production-scale data.

Django's 'GIS integration via GeoDjango' provides geographic model fields and spatial queries. Enable: add 'django.contrib.gis' to INSTALLED_APPS; use PostgreSQL with PostGIS extension. Define: 'location = models.PointField()'. Spatial queries: 'Place.objects.filter(location__distance_lte=(point, D(km=5)))'. 'GeoQuerySet' methods: 'geojson()' for GeoJSON output, 'transform(4326)' for coordinate system transformation. For geofencing: 'filter(location__within=polygon)'. GeoDjango requires GDAL/GEOS libraries — ensure they're in Docker images. For simpler distance calculations without PostGIS, use the Haversine formula in Python, but PostGIS is orders of magnitude faster for large datasets.

Django's 'transaction savepoint release' semantics are database-specific and unintuitive. Releasing a savepoint in PostgreSQL does NOT commit the data — it just removes the savepoint marker. The data is committed when the outermost transaction commits. 'with transaction.atomic(): savepoint_id = transaction.savepoint(); ...; transaction.savepoint_commit(savepoint_id)' — the commit here means 'proceed, don't rollback to this savepoint' not 'commit to database'. 'transaction.savepoint_rollback(savepoint_id)' rolls back to the savepoint state. This distinction matters when debugging transaction behavior: seeing 'savepoint_commit()' in code does not mean the transaction is committed.

Django's 'django-cacheops' enables automatic queryset caching with smart invalidation. Unlike manual '@cache_page', cacheops automatically invalidates cached queries when related models are saved. Configure: 'CACHEOPS = {"auth.User": {"ops": "all", "timeout": 60*15}, "myapp.*": {"ops": "all", "timeout": 60*60}}'. Use '@cached_as' decorator for complex cache keys. 'cacheops_invalidate(MyModel, pk=1)' for manual invalidation. Cacheops works at ORM level — caches 'QuerySet.filter(...).values()' results keyed by the SQL query. For high-read, low-write models (categories, settings, translations), aggressive caching with cacheops can reduce database load by 90%+.

Django's 'SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER' must be configured when behind a load balancer for 'request.is_secure()' to return True. Without this, 'request.is_secure()' always returns False even for HTTPS traffic, breaking: SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE (secure cookies), redirect checks, 'HttpRequest.build_absolute_uri()'. Configure: 'SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER = ("HTTP_X_FORWARDED_PROTO", "https")' if the load balancer sends this header. Only set this if you control and trust the proxy — a malicious client could spoof this header. Combine with 'SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT = True' for full HTTPS enforcement.

Django's 'django-simple-history' enables automatic model change history tracking. 'class Article(models.Model): history = HistoricalRecords()'. Each save creates a snapshot in a separate 'HistoricalArticle' table. Access history: 'article.history.all()' returns all historical records. Diff between versions: 'old_record = article.history.get(pk=old_pk); new_record = article.history.get(pk=new_pk); delta = new_record.diff_against(old_record)'. Restore old version: 'old_version.instance.save()'. The 'history_user' tracks who made each change — set via middleware or manually: 'with set_current_user(request.user): article.save()'. Use for user-facing audit trails and admin review of changes.

Django's 'ORM aggregate with GROUP BY' behavior is non-obvious when combining with annotations. 'User.objects.annotate(order_count=Count("orders")).filter(order_count__gt=5)' works. But 'User.objects.annotate(order_count=Count("orders")).filter(order_count__gt=5, orders__status="paid")' adds a WHERE clause BEFORE aggregation, changing the semantics: it counts only paid orders (not total) and only returns users who have at least one paid order. Use 'Subquery' or 'filter()' before 'annotate()' for pre-aggregation filtering vs 'filter()' after 'annotate()' for post-aggregation filtering. Django generates different SQL for filter position relative to annotate.

Django's 'django.contrib.postgres.aggregates' provides PostgreSQL-specific aggregation functions not available in standard SQL. 'ArrayAgg': collect values into an array — 'User.objects.annotate(tag_list=ArrayAgg("tags__name"))'. 'StringAgg': concatenate strings — 'annotate(csv_names=StringAgg("name", delimiter=",", ordering="name"))'. 'JSONBAgg': aggregate as JSONB array. 'BitAnd', 'BitOr': bitwise aggregation. These enable complex aggregation in a single SQL query that would otherwise require Python-side processing of multiple querysets. 'ArrayAgg(distinct=True)' deduplicates. Combined with 'filter' parameter on aggregation functions: 'Count("orders", filter=Q(orders__status="paid"))' for conditional aggregation.
